title.zh: 卷四十一 樊酈滕灌傅靳周傳
title.en: Volume 41: Fan, Li, Teng, Guan, Fu, Jin and Zhou
title.raw: 卷四十一 樊酈滕灌傅靳周傳 Volume 41: Fan, Li, Teng, Guan, Fu, Jin and Zhou

sentences.zh: 樊噲，沛人也，以屠狗為事。

translations.literal: Fan Kuai was a man of Pei, and made his living by dog-butcher trade.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Kuai came from Pei and earned his keep as a dog butcher.

sentences.zh: 後與高祖俱隱於芒碭山澤間。

translations.literal: Afterward he together with the High Ancestor both hid among the marshes and hills of Mang and Dang.
translations.idiomatic: Later he went into hiding with the High Ancestor in the marshes and hills between Mang and Dang.

sentences.zh: 陳勝初起，蕭何、曹參使噲求迎高祖，立為沛公。

translations.literal: When Chen Sheng first rose, Xiao He and Cao Can sent Kuai to seek and welcome the High Ancestor, who was then installed as Lord of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: When Chen Sheng first raised rebellion, Xiao He and Cao Can dispatched Kuai to find the High Ancestor and escort him back; he was thereupon proclaimed Lord of Pei.

sentences.zh: 噲以舍人從攻胡陵、方與，還守豐，擊泗水監豐下，破之。

translations.literal: Kuai, as a house-guest retainer, followed in the attack on Huling and Fangyu, returned to guard Feng, struck the Sishui intendant below Feng, and defeated him.
translations.idiomatic: Serving as a house-guest, Kuai joined the assault on Huling and Fangyu, then turned back to hold Feng, where he attacked the Sishui intendant beneath the town and broke his force.

sentences.zh: 復東定沛，破泗水守薛西。

translations.literal: Again he went east and secured Pei, and defeated the Sishui governor west of Xue.
translations.idiomatic: He then marched east again, pacified Pei, and routed the Sishui governor west of Xue.

sentences.zh: 與司馬镛戰碭東，卻敵，斬首十五級，賜爵國大夫。

translations.literal: He fought with Sima Yong east of Dang, drove back the enemy, beheaded fifteen heads, and was granted the rank of grandee of the state.
translations.idiomatic: East of Dang he engaged Sima Yong, threw back the foe, took fifteen heads, and received the noble rank of grandee of the state.

sentences.zh: 常從，沛公擊章邯軍濮陽，攻城先登，斬首二十三級，賜爵列大夫。

translations.literal: Constantly following, when Lord Pei attacked Zhang Han’s army at Puyang, he was first to scale the walls, beheaded twenty-three heads, and was granted the rank of grandee of the columns.
translations.idiomatic: Ever in attendance, he was with Lord Pei at the siege of Zhang Han’s host at Puyang: he led the escalade, claimed twenty-three heads, and was ennobled as grandee of the columns.

sentences.zh: 從攻陽城，先登。

translations.literal: He followed in the attack on Yangcheng, being first to ascend.
translations.idiomatic: He joined the strike on Yangcheng and was again the first man over the wall.

sentences.zh: 下戶牖，破李由軍，斬首十六級，賜上聞爵。

translations.literal: He took Huyou, destroyed Li You’s army, beheaded sixteen heads, and was granted the rank of grandee of superior report.
translations.idiomatic: He reduced Huyou, shattered Li You’s troops, took sixteen heads, and was awarded the rank of grandee of superior report.

sentences.zh: 後攻圉都尉、東郡守尉於成武，卻敵，斬首十四級，捕虜十六人，賜爵五大夫。

translations.literal: Later he attacked the Woyu commandant and the Dongjun assistant governor at Chengwu, drove back the enemy, beheaded fourteen heads, captured sixteen prisoners, and was granted the rank of fifth-order grandee.
translations.idiomatic: Later, at Chengwu, he struck the Woyu commandant and the Dongjun assistant governor, repulsed their line, cut off fourteen heads, seized sixteen captives, and rose to fifth-order grandee.

sentences.zh: 從攻秦軍，出亳南。

translations.literal: He followed in attacking Qin troops, emerging south of Bo.
translations.idiomatic: He followed the offensive against Qin forces and debouched south of Bo.

sentences.zh: 河間守軍於杠里，破之。

translations.literal: The Hejian governor encamped his army at Gangli; Kuai defeated him.
translations.idiomatic: The Hejian governor had drawn up at Gangli; Kuai smashed that camp.

sentences.zh: 擊破趙賁軍開封北，以卻敵先登，斬候一人，首六十八級，捕虜二十六人，賜爵卿。

translations.literal: He struck and destroyed Zhao Fen’s army north of Kaifeng; as one who drove back the foe and was first to ascend, he beheaded one marquis, sixty-eight heads, captured twenty-six prisoners, and was granted the rank of ministerial grandee.
translations.idiomatic: North of Kaifeng he broke Zhao Fen’s host; for throwing back the enemy and leading the escalade he slew a marquis, piled up sixty-eight heads, took twenty-six prisoners, and was raised to ministerial grandee.

sentences.zh: 從攻破揚熊於曲遇。

translations.literal: He followed in defeating Yang Xiong at Quyu.
translations.idiomatic: He took part in the victory over Yang Xiong at Quyu.

sentences.zh: 攻宛陵，先登，斬首八級，捕虜四十四人，賜爵封號賢成君。

translations.literal: He attacked Wanling, being first to ascend, beheaded eight heads, captured forty-four prisoners, and was granted a fief and the title Worthy Accomplished Lord.
translations.idiomatic: At Wanling he again topped the wall, claimed eight heads and forty-four captives, and received a patent as Worthy Accomplished Lord.

sentences.zh: 從攻長社、轘轅，絕河津，東攻秦軍尸鄉，南攻秦軍於犨。

translations.literal: He followed in attacking Changshe and Huanyuan, cut the ferry on the Yellow River, attacked eastward Qin troops at Shixiang, and attacked southward Qin troops at Chou.
translations.idiomatic: He marched on Changshe and Huanyuan, severed the river crossing, then struck Qin columns at Shixiang to the east and at Chou to the south.

sentences.zh: 破南陽守齮於陽城。

translations.literal: He defeated the Nanyang governor Qi at Yangcheng.
translations.idiomatic: South of Yangcheng he defeated Nanyang Governor Qi.

sentences.zh: 東攻宛城，先登。

translations.literal: He attacked eastward on Wancheng, being first to ascend.
translations.idiomatic: He turned east against Wancheng and was first to scale it.

sentences.zh: 西至酈，以卻敵，斬首十四級，捕虜四十四人，賜重封。

translations.literal: Westward to Li, driving back the enemy, he beheaded fourteen heads, captured forty-four prisoners, and was granted a double enfeoffment.
translations.idiomatic: West to Li he again repelled the enemy, added fourteen heads and forty-four prisoners, and earned a second grant of fief.

sentences.zh: 攻武關，至霸上，斬都尉一人，首十級，捕虜百四十六人，降卒二千九百人。

translations.literal: He attacked Wuguan, reached Bashang, beheaded one commandant-in-chief, ten heads, captured one hundred forty-six prisoners, and caused two thousand nine hundred surrendered troops to yield.
translations.idiomatic: He forced Wuguan, advanced to Bashang, slew a commandant-in-chief and ten others, took one hundred forty-six living prisoners, and accepted the capitulation of two thousand nine hundred men.

sentences.zh: 項羽在戲下，欲攻沛公。

translations.literal: Xiang Yu was below Xi and wished to attack Lord Pei.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu lay encamped below the Xi ford and planned to fall upon Lord Pei.

sentences.zh: 沛公從百餘騎因項伯面見項羽，謝無有閉關事。

translations.literal: Lord Pei, with a hundred-odd horsemen, through Xiang Bo obtained a face-to-face meeting with Xiang Yu and apologized that there had been no affair of closing the passes.
translations.idiomatic: Lord Pei rode in with little more than a hundred horse, used Xiang Bo’s good offices to gain an audience with Xiang Yu, and disclaimed any intent to seal the passes against him.

sentences.zh: 項羽既饗軍土，中酒，亞父謀欲殺沛公，令項莊拔劍舞坐中，欲擊沛公，項伯常屏蔽之。

translations.literal: Xiang Yu had already feasted his officers and men; mid-banquet the Second Father plotted to kill Lord Pei, ordered Xiang Zhuang to draw his sword and dance in the midst of the seats intending to strike Lord Pei, and Xiang Bo constantly screened him with his own body.
translations.idiomatic: Once Xiang Yu had feasted his army and wine flowed freely, the Second Father schemed to cut down Lord Pei: he had Xiang Zhuang rise with sword in hand and dance among the couches to get a stroke at Lord Pei, while Xiang Bo again and again interposed his own frame.

sentences.zh: 時獨沛公與張良得入坐，樊噲居營外，聞事急，乃持盾入。

translations.literal: At that time only Lord Pei and Zhang Liang were admitted to sit; Fan Kuai was outside the camp; hearing the matter was urgent, he then took his shield and entered.
translations.idiomatic: Only Lord Pei and Zhang Liang had seats within; Fan Kuai waited outside the lines. When he heard how desperate things had become, he seized his shield and forced his way in.

sentences.zh: 初入營，營衛止噲，噲直撞入，立帳下。

translations.literal: First entering the camp, the camp guards tried to stop Kuai; Kuai straightway shouldered through and stood beneath the curtain.
translations.idiomatic: The sentries tried to bar him at the gate; he rammed straight through and took his stand below Xiang Yu’s tent.

sentences.zh: 項羽目之，問為誰。

translations.literal: Xiang Yu looked at him and asked who he was.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu fixed him with a stare and demanded his name.

sentences.zh: 張良曰：「沛公參乘樊噲也。」

translations.literal: Zhang Liang said: “This is Fan Kuai, Lord Pei’s chariot-side guard.”
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang answered, “Fan Kuai, who rides at Lord Pei’s right hand in the chariot.”

sentences.zh: 項羽曰：「壯士。」

translations.literal: Xiang Yu said: “A brave fellow.”
translations.idiomatic: “A brave man,” said Xiang Yu.

sentences.zh: 賜之卮酒彘肩。

translations.literal: He gave him a goblet of wine and a shoulder of pork.
translations.idiomatic: He sent him a cup of wine and a shoulder of boar.

sentences.zh: 噲既飲酒，拔劍切肉食之。

translations.literal: Kuai, having drunk the wine, drew his sword, cut the meat, and ate it.
translations.idiomatic: Kuai tossed off the wine, whipped out his blade, carved the meat, and ate it where he stood.

sentences.zh: 項羽曰：「能復飲乎？」

translations.literal: Xiang Yu said: “Can you drink again?”
translations.idiomatic: “Will you have another cup?” asked Xiang Yu.

sentences.zh: 噲曰：「臣死且不辭，豈特卮酒乎！

translations.literal: Kuai said: “I would not decline even death; how much less a mere cup of wine!
translations.idiomatic: Kuai replied, “I would not refuse death itself—why should I balk at a cup of wine?

sentences.zh: 且沛公先入定咸陽，暴師霸上，以待大王。

translations.literal: Moreover Lord Pei was first to enter and settle Xianyang; he exposed his host at Bashang to await the Great King.
translations.idiomatic: Lord Pei was the first to march into Xianyang and bring order there; he bivouacked his army at Bashang only to welcome Your Majesty.

sentences.zh: 大王今日至，聽小人之言，與沛公有隙，臣恐天下解心疑大王也。」

translations.literal: The Great King has come today; listening to petty men’s words, you have opened a breach with Lord Pei; your servant fears that all under Heaven will lose heart and begin to doubt the Great King.”
translations.idiomatic: Yet now that you have arrived you heed slander and turn against him; I fear the realm will lose faith in you, Great King.”

sentences.zh: 項羽默然。

translations.literal: Xiang Yu was silent.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu had nothing to say.

sentences.zh: 沛公如廁，麾噲去。

translations.literal: Lord Pei went to the privy and signaled Kuai to leave.
translations.idiomatic: Lord Pei rose as if for the latrine and motioned Kuai to follow him out.

sentences.zh: 既出，沛公留車騎，獨騎馬，噲等四人步從，從山下走歸霸上軍，而使張良謝項羽。

translations.literal: Having gone out, Lord Pei left his chariots and horse escort, rode alone on one horse, while Kuai and four others followed on foot; they went down the hill by footpaths and returned to the Bashang army, while he sent Zhang Liang to make his apologies to Xiang Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Outside, Lord Pei abandoned his carriage train, mounted a single horse, and with Kuai and four others jogging afoot took the bypaths down the hill back to his camp at Bashang, dispatching Zhang Liang to tender his excuses to Xiang Yu.

sentences.zh: 羽亦因遂已，無誅沛公之心。

translations.literal: Yu also thereupon let the matter drop and had no further mind to execute Lord Pei.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu likewise dropped the design and no longer thought of killing Lord Pei.

sentences.zh: 是日微樊噲奔入營譙讓項羽，沛公幾殆。

translations.literal: On that day, had not Fan Kuai burst into the camp to rebuke Xiang Yu, Lord Pei would almost have perished.
translations.idiomatic: That day, but for Fan Kuai’s rush into the lines to upbraid Xiang Yu, Lord Pei would have been lost.

sentences.zh: 後數日，項羽入屠咸陽，立沛公為漢王。

translations.literal: Several days later Xiang Yu entered and massacred Xianyang, and installed Lord Pei as King of Han.
translations.idiomatic: A few days afterward Xiang Yu marched into Xianyang and put the city to the sword, then invested Lord Pei as King of Han.

sentences.zh: 漢王賜噲爵為列侯，號臨武侯。

translations.literal: The King of Han granted Kuai a marquis’s rank with the title Marquis of Linwu.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han ennobled Kuai as a full marquis under the style Marquis of Linwu.

sentences.zh: 遷為郎中，從入漢中。

translations.literal: He was promoted to gentleman-of-the-palace and followed in entering Hanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: He was advanced to gentleman-of-the-palace and accompanied the court into Hanzhong.

sentences.zh: 還定三秦，別擊西丞白水北，擁輕車騎雍南，破之。

translations.literal: On the return march that pacified the Three Qin, he separately struck the western assistant magistrate north of Baishui, intercepted light chariots and cavalry south of Yong, and defeated them.
translations.idiomatic: When the army turned back to settle the Three Qin, he led a detached column against the western assistant north of Baishui, cut off light chariots and horse south of Yong, and broke both forces.

sentences.zh: 從攻雍、焘城，先登。

translations.literal: He followed in attacking Yong and Taocheng, being first to ascend.
translations.idiomatic: He joined the assault on Yong and on Taocheng and again led the escalade.

sentences.zh: 擊章平軍好畤，攻城，先登陷陣，斬縣令丞各一人，首十一級，虜二十人，遷為郎中騎將。

translations.literal: He struck Zhang Ping’s army at Haozhi, attacked the city, was first to ascend and pierce the line, beheaded one county magistrate and one assistant magistrate each, eleven heads, captured twenty prisoners, and was promoted to chief of mounted gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: At Haozhi he hit Zhang Ping’s host, stormed the walls, was first over the ramparts and through the formation, slew a magistrate and his deputy, took eleven heads and twenty prisoners, and rose to chief of mounted gentlemen.

sentences.zh: 從擊秦車騎壤東，卻敵，遷為將軍。

translations.literal: He followed in striking Qin chariots and cavalry east of Rang, drove back the enemy, and was promoted to general.
translations.idiomatic: East of Rang he engaged Qin chariotry and cavalry, threw them back, and was promoted to general.

sentences.zh: 攻趙賁，下郿、槐里、柳中、咸陽；

translations.literal: He attacked Zhao Fen and took Mei, Huaili, Liuzhong, and Xianyang.
translations.idiomatic: He stormed Zhao Fen’s positions and reduced Mei, Huaili, Liuzhong, and Xianyang.

sentences.zh: 灌廢丘，最。

translations.literal: he flooded Feiqiu and ranked highest.
translations.idiomatic: he drowned the defenders of Feiqiu and earned the highest merit rating.

sentences.zh: 至櫟陽，賜食邑杜之樊鄉。

translations.literal: When he reached Liyang he was granted the village of Fan in Du as a food fief.
translations.idiomatic: At Liyang he received as sustenance fief the hamlet of Fan in Du.

sentences.zh: 從攻項籍，屠煮棗，擊破王武、程處軍於外黃。

translations.literal: He followed in attacking Xiang Ji, massacred Zhuzao, and broke the armies of Wang Wu and Cheng Chu at Waihuang.
translations.idiomatic: He campaigned against Xiang Ji, sacked Zhuzao, and shattered the hosts of Wang Wu and Cheng Chu at Waihuang.

sentences.zh: 攻鄒、魯、瑕丘、薛。

translations.literal: He attacked Zou, Lu, Xiaqiu, and Xue.
translations.idiomatic: He struck Zou, Lu, Xiaqiu, and Xue.

sentences.zh: 項羽敗漢王於彭城，盡復取魯、梁地。

translations.literal: Xiang Yu defeated the King of Han at Pengcheng and wholly recovered Lu and Liang.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu crushed the King of Han at Pengcheng and reconquered the whole of Lu and Liang.

sentences.zh: 噲還至滎陽，益食平陰二千戶，以將軍守廣武一歲。

translations.literal: Kuai returned to Xingyang; his food income was increased by two thousand households at Pingyin, and as a general he held Guangwu for one year.
translations.idiomatic: Kuai fell back to Xingyang, had two thousand more households at Pingyin added to his income, and for a year defended Guangwu as a general.

sentences.zh: 項羽引東，從高祖擊項籍，下陽夏，虜楚周將軍卒四千人。

translations.literal: When Xiang Yu drew eastward, he followed the High Ancestor in striking Xiang Ji, reduced Yangxia, and captured four thousand men of Chu’s General Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiang Yu withdrew east, he marched with the High Ancestor against Xiang Ji, took Yangxia, and rounded up four thousand soldiers of Chu’s General Zhou.

sentences.zh: 圍項籍陳，大破之。

translations.literal: He besieged Xiang Ji at Chen and heavily defeated him.
translations.idiomatic: He helped ring Xiang Ji at Chen and dealt him a crushing defeat.

sentences.zh: 屠胡陵。

translations.literal: He massacred Huling.
translations.idiomatic: He put Huling to the sword.

sentences.zh: 項籍死，漢王即皇帝位，以噲有功，益食邑八百戶。

translations.literal: When Xiang Ji died, the King of Han assumed the imperial title; because Kuai had merit, his food fief was increased by eight hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: After Xiang Ji’s death the King of Han mounted the throne; for Kuai’s services another eight hundred households were added to his fief.

sentences.zh: 其秋，燕王臧荼反，噲從攻虜荼，定燕地。

translations.literal: That autumn the King of Yan Zang Tu rebelled; Kuai followed in the attack, captured Tu, and settled the Yan territory.
translations.idiomatic: The same autumn King Zang Tu of Yan rose in revolt; Kuai joined the expedition, took Zang Tu prisoner, and pacified Yan.

sentences.zh: 楚王韓信反，噲從至陳，取信，定楚。

translations.literal: When King Xin of Chu rebelled, Kuai followed to Chen, seized Xin, and settled Chu.
translations.idiomatic: When the King of Han, Xin, rebelled, Kuai accompanied the host to Chen, seized Han Xin, and brought Chu to heel.

sentences.zh: 更賜爵列侯，與剖符，世世勿絕，食舞陽，號為舞陽侯，除前所食。

translations.literal: His noble rank was again changed to full marquis; he was given a tally-splitting patent, perpetual through the generations, fed at Wuyang, titled Marquis of Wuyang, and his previous grants were rescinded.
translations.idiomatic: He was re-enfeoffed as a full marquis with a split tally meant never to lapse, with income centered on Wuyang and the style Marquis of Wuyang, superseding his earlier holdings.

sentences.zh: 以將軍從攻反者韓王信於代。

translations.literal: As a general he followed in attacking the rebel King of Han Xin in Dai.
translations.idiomatic: As general he joined the campaign against the rebel King of Han Xin in Dai.

sentences.zh: 自霍人以往至雲中，與絳侯等共定之，益食千五百戶。

translations.literal: From Huoren westward to Yunzhong he together with the Marquis of Zhou and others jointly settled the region; his food fief was increased by one thousand five hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: From Huoren out to Yunzhong he and the Marquis of Zhou and the rest jointly pacified the commanderies, and fifteen hundred more households were added to his fief.

sentences.zh: 因擊陳狶與曼丘臣軍，戰襄國，破柏人，先登，降之定清河、常山凡二十七縣，殘東垣，遷為左丞相。

translations.literal: He then struck Chen Xi and the army of Manqiu Chen, fought at Xiangguo, broke Bairen, was first to ascend, received the surrender, and settled Qinghe and Changshan in all twenty-seven counties; he ravaged Dongyuan and was promoted to left chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: He next hit Chen Xi and Manqiu Chen’s force, fought at Xiangguo, stormed Bairen, led the escalade, accepted their surrender, and brought twenty-seven counties of Qinghe and Changshan to submission; he razed Dongyuan and was named left chancellor.

sentences.zh: 破得綦母印、尹潘軍於無終、廣昌。

translations.literal: He defeated the armies of Qimu Yin and Yin Pan at Wuzhong and Guangchang.
translations.idiomatic: At Wuzhong and Guangchang he broke the hosts of Qimu Yin and Yin Pan.

sentences.zh: 破豨別將胡人王黃軍代南，因擊韓信軍參合。

translations.literal: He defeated Chen Xi’s detached general, the Hu barbarian Wang Huang’s army south of Dai, then struck Han Xin’s army at Shenhe.
translations.idiomatic: He smashed the Hu commander Wang Huang, a detached general of Chen Xi, south of Dai, then fell upon Han Xin’s troops at Shenhe.

sentences.zh: 軍所將卒斬韓信，擊豨胡騎橫谷，斬將軍趙既，虜代丞相馮梁、守孫奮、大將王黃、將軍大將一人、太僕解福等十人。

translations.literal: The troops he led beheaded Han Xin, struck Chen Xi’s Hu cavalry at Henggu, beheaded General Zhao Ji, captured Dai chancellor Feng Liang, defender Sun Fen, grand general Wang Huang, one general-in-chief, grand coachman Xie Fu, and ten others in all.
translations.idiomatic: The soldiers under his command cut down Han Xin, rode down Chen Xi’s Hu horse at Henggu, slew General Zhao Ji, and took Dai Chancellor Feng Liang, Defender Sun Fen, Grand General Wang Huang, one general-in-chief, Grand Coachman Xie Fu, and ten captives besides.

sentences.zh: 與諸將共定代鄉邑七十三。

translations.literal: Together with the various generals he settled seventy-three villages and towns of Dai.
translations.idiomatic: With the other commanders he reduced seventy-three townships of Dai.

sentences.zh: 後燕王盧綰反，噲以相國擊綰，破其丞相抵薊南，定燕縣十八，鄉邑五十一。

translations.literal: Later when King Lu Wan of Yan rebelled, Kuai as chancellor of state struck Wan, defeated his chancellor Di south of Ji, and settled eighteen counties of Yan and fifty-one villages and towns.
translations.idiomatic: Later, when Lu Wan king of Yan revolted, Kuai as chancellor of state marched against him, broke Wan’s chancellor Di south of Ji, and secured eighteen Yan counties and fifty-one townships.

sentences.zh: 益食千三百戶，定食舞陽五千四百戶。

translations.literal: His food fief was increased by one thousand three hundred households; his settled income at Wuyang was five thousand four hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: Thirteen hundred more households were added to his income; his permanent grant at Wuyang stood at five thousand four hundred households.

sentences.zh: 從，斬首百七十六級，虜二百八十七人。

translations.literal: On campaigns where he followed the main host he beheaded one hundred seventy-six and captured two hundred eighty-seven.
translations.idiomatic: In the battles where he marched with the main army he took one hundred seventy-six heads and two hundred eighty-seven prisoners.

sentences.zh: 別，破軍七，下城五，定郡六，縣五十二，得丞相一人，將軍十三人，二千石以下至三百石十二人。

translations.literal: On separate commands he destroyed seven armies, took five cities, settled six commanderies, fifty-two counties, captured one chancellor, thirteen generals, and twelve officials from rank of two thousand piculs down to three hundred piculs.
translations.idiomatic: On independent operations he broke seven field armies, seized five walled towns, pacified six commanderies and fifty-two counties, and brought in one chancellor, thirteen generals, and twelve officers from two-thousand-picul rank down to three hundred piculs.

sentences.zh: 噲以呂后弟呂須為婦，生子伉，故其比諸將最親。

translations.literal: Kuai took as wife Lü Xu, younger sister of Empress Lü, and begot a son Kang; therefore among the generals he was the closest.
translations.idiomatic: Kuai had married Lü Xu, the empress’s younger sister, and fathered Kang, so he stood nearer to the throne than any other commander.

sentences.zh: 先黥布反時，高帝嘗病，惡見人，臥禁中，詔戶者無得入群臣。

translations.literal: Earlier, when Qing Bu rebelled, the High Emperor was once ill, loathed seeing people, lay in the forbidden quarters, and ordered the doorkeepers to let no host of ministers enter.
translations.idiomatic: When Qing Bu first rose, the High Emperor had fallen ill, could not bear the sight of anyone, and lay secluded in the inner palace with orders that no minister be admitted.

sentences.zh: 群臣絳、灌等莫敢入。

translations.literal: Among the ministers Zhou, Guan, and the like none dared enter.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Bo, Guan Ying, and the rest of the ministers none dared cross the threshold.

sentences.zh: 十餘日，噲乃排闥直入，大臣隨之。

translations.literal: After more than ten days Kuai then pushed open the doors straight in, and the great ministers followed him.
translations.idiomatic: After a fortnight Kuai shouldered the doors open and strode in, and the senior officials trailed behind.

sentences.zh: 上獨枕一宦者臥。

translations.literal: The emperor alone lay with his head on one eunuch’s lap.
translations.idiomatic: The sovereign was resting with his head pillowed on a single eunuch’s knee.

sentences.zh: 噲等見上流涕曰：「始陛下與臣等起豐沛，定天下，何其壯也！

translations.literal: Kuai and the others, seeing the emperor, wept with flowing tears and said: “At first Your Majesty with your servants rose from Feng and Pei and settled the empire—how bold that was!
translations.idiomatic: Kuai and his companions, finding him thus, wept and said, “When you and we first rose from Feng and Pei to win the realm, what vigor you showed!

sentences.zh: 今天下已定，又何憊也！

translations.literal: Today the empire is already settled; why are you so weary!
translations.idiomatic: The realm is now at peace—why do you show such exhaustion?

sentences.zh: 且陛下病甚，大臣震恐，不見臣等計事，顧獨與一宦者絕乎？

translations.literal: Moreover Your Majesty’s illness is severe; the great ministers tremble with fear; not meeting your servants to deliberate affairs, do you intend to cut yourself off with a single eunuch alone?
translations.idiomatic: Your illness is grave; the ministers are terrified; yet you refuse council and closet yourself with one eunuch alone—

sentences.zh: 且陛下獨不見趙高之事乎？」

translations.literal: Moreover does Your Majesty alone not see what happened in the case of Zhao Gao?”
translations.idiomatic: have you forgotten how Zhao Gao brought down the Second Emperor of Qin?”

sentences.zh: 高帝笑而起。

translations.literal: The High Emperor laughed and rose.
translations.idiomatic: The High Emperor laughed and got up from his couch.

sentences.zh: 其後盧綰反，高帝使噲以相國擊燕。

translations.literal: Later when Lu Wan rebelled, the High Emperor ordered Kuai as chancellor of state to strike Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Later, when Lu Wan rebelled, the High Emperor sent Kuai as chancellor of state to campaign in Yan.

sentences.zh: 是時高帝病甚，人有惡噲黨於呂氏，即上一日宮車晏駕，則噲欲以兵盡誅戚氏、趙王如意之屬。

translations.literal: At that time the High Emperor was gravely ill; someone slandered Kuai as belonging to the Lü faction, saying that if the sovereign should one day die in the palace carriage, then Kuai wished to use troops to slaughter to the last the Lady Qi clansmen and King Zhao Ruyi and their kind.
translations.idiomatic: By then the emperor was dying; men accused Kuai of siding with the Lüs, claiming that the moment the palace carriage should halt forever Kuai meant to move the army and exterminate Lady Qi’s kin, King Ruyi of Zhao, and all their following.

sentences.zh: 高帝大怒，乃使陳平載絳侯代將，而即軍中斬噲。

translations.literal: The High Emperor in great rage then ordered Chen Ping to convey the Marquis of Zhou to replace him as commander and behead Kuai in the army on the spot.
translations.idiomatic: In a fury he ordered Chen Ping to ride with the Marquis of Zhou to supersede Kuai’s command and execute him in camp.

sentences.zh: 陳平畏呂后，執噲詣長安。

translations.literal: Chen Ping feared Empress Lü; he seized Kuai and escorted him to Chang’an.
translations.idiomatic: Chen Ping dreaded Empress Lü, so he bound Kuai instead and hauled him toward Chang’an.

sentences.zh: 至則高帝已崩，呂后釋噲，得復爵邑。

translations.literal: When he arrived the High Emperor had already died; Empress Lü released Kuai, and he recovered his noble rank and fief.
translations.idiomatic: By the time they arrived the High Emperor was gone; Empress Lü freed Kuai, who regained his title and lands.

sentences.zh: 孝惠六年，噲薨，諡曰武侯，子伉嗣。

translations.literal: In the sixth year of Emperor Hui, Kuai died; he was posthumously titled Martial Marquis, and his son Kang succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: In the sixth year of Emperor Hui’s reign Kuai died, receiving the posthumous epithet Martial Marquis; his heir was Kang.

sentences.zh: 而伉母呂須亦為臨光侯，噲高后時用事顓權，大臣盡畏之。

translations.literal: Moreover Kang’s mother Lü Xu was also made Marquis of Liguang, and in the time of Kuai and the Empress Dowager Lü they wielded power and monopolized authority; the great ministers all feared them.
translations.idiomatic: Kang’s mother Lü Xu was herself ennobled as Marquis of Liguang, and while Kuai and Empress Dowager Lü held sway they ran the government unchecked; every senior minister walked in fear of them.

sentences.zh: 高后崩，大臣誅呂須等，因誅伉，舞陽侯中絕數月。

translations.literal: When the Empress Dowager Lü died, the great ministers executed Lü Xu and the rest, and therefore executed Kang; the marquisate of Wuyang was cut off for several months.
translations.idiomatic: After the empress dowager’s death the ministers put Lü Xu and her faction to the sword, Kang with them, and the line of Marquis of Wuyang lapsed for some months.

sentences.zh: 孝文帝立，乃復封噲庶子市人為侯，復故邑。

translations.literal: When Emperor Wen ascended the throne, he again enfeoffed Kuai’s son by a concubine, Shiren, as marquis, restoring the old fief.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wen then reinstated Kuai’s bastard son Shiren as marquis with the original estate.

sentences.zh: 薨，諡曰荒侯。

translations.literal: He died and was posthumously titled Reckless Marquis.
translations.idiomatic: When he died he was given the posthumous name Reckless Marquis.

sentences.zh: 子佗廣嗣。

translations.literal: His son Tuoguang succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His son Tuoguang inherited the title.

sentences.zh: 六歲，其舍人上書言：「荒侯巿人病不能為人，令其夫人與其弟亂而生佗廣，佗廣實非荒侯子。」

translations.literal: In the sixth year of his marquisate a house-guest submitted a memorial saying: “Marquis Reckless Shiren was ill and unable to perform as a man; he ordered his wife to commit incest with his younger brother, by whom Tuoguang was born; Tuoguang is in truth not the son of Marquis Reckless.”
translations.idiomatic: In the sixth year of Tuoguang’s tenure as marquis a retainer memorialized: “Marquis Reckless Shiren was impotent; he had his wife lie with his younger brother, who begot Tuoguang—Tuoguang is not truly the marquis’s son.”

sentences.zh: 下吏，免。

translations.literal: The case was handed to the judges; he was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: The matter went to the law officers; the title was stripped.

sentences.zh: 平帝元始二年，繼絕世，封噲玄孫之子章為舞陽侯，邑千戶。

translations.literal: In the second year of Emperor Ping’s Yuanshi era, continuing severed lines, he enfeoffed Zhang, a son of Kuai’s great-great-grandson’s son, as Marquis of Wuyang, with a fief of one thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanshi 2 of Emperor Ping, when broken noble lines were revived, Zhang—a descendant in the fifth generation from Kuai—was made Marquis of Wuyang with a thousand-household estate.

sentences.zh: 酈商，高陽人也。

translations.literal: Li Shang was a man of Gaoyang.
translations.idiomatic: Li Shang came from Gaoyang.

sentences.zh: 陳勝起，商聚少年得數千人。

translations.literal: When Chen Sheng rose, Shang gathered young men and obtained several thousand.
translations.idiomatic: When Chen Sheng rebelled, Shang rallied youths until he had several thousand under him.

sentences.zh: 沛公略地六月餘，商以所將四千人屬沛公於岐。

translations.literal: For more than six months Lord Pei overran the land; Shang with the four thousand he led attached himself to Lord Pei at Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Lord Pei had been reducing territory for over half a year when Shang brought the four thousand troops he commanded and joined him at Qi.

sentences.zh: 從攻長社，先登，賜爵封信成君。

translations.literal: He followed in attacking Changshe, being first to ascend, and was granted the rank and title Lord Trustworthy Accomplished.
translations.idiomatic: He fought at Changshe, was first over the wall, and was ennobled as Lord Trustworthy Accomplished.

sentences.zh: 從攻緱氏，絕河津，破秦軍雒陽東。

translations.literal: He followed in attacking Goushi, cut the ferry on the Yellow River, and destroyed Qin troops east of Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: He marched on Goushi, severed the Yellow River crossing, and broke Qin forces east of Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 從下宛、穰，定十七縣。

translations.literal: He followed in reducing Wan and Rang, settling seventeen counties.
translations.idiomatic: He helped take Wan and Rang and brought seventeen counties to heel.

sentences.zh: 別將攻旬關，西定漢中。

translations.literal: As a detached commander he attacked Xunguan and westward settled Hanzhong.
translations.idiomatic: Leading a separate column he struck Xunguan and secured Hanzhong to the west.

sentences.zh: 沛公為漢王，賜商爵信成君，以將軍為隴西都尉。

translations.literal: Lord Pei became King of Han; he granted Shang the noble rank Lord Trustworthy Accomplished and appointed him as colonel and metropolitan governor of Longxi.
translations.idiomatic: When Lord Pei became King of Han, Shang kept the title Lord Trustworthy Accomplished and was named general and Longxi metropolitan governor.

sentences.zh: 別定北地郡，破章邯別將於烏氏、栒邑、泥陽，賜食邑武城六千戶。

translations.literal: Separately he settled Beidi commandery, defeated Zhang Han’s detached general at Wushi, Xunyi, and Niyang, and was granted a food fief at Wucheng of six thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: On his own he pacified Beidi, broke a detached column of Zhang Han’s at Wushi, Xunyi, and Niyang, and received six thousand households at Wucheng.

sentences.zh: 從擊項籍軍，與鍾離眛戰，受梁相國印，益食四千戶。

translations.literal: He followed in striking Xiang Ji’s army, fought with Zhongli Mo, received the seal of chancellor of state of the Liang kingdom, and his food income was increased by four thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: He campaigned against Xiang Ji, crossed swords with Zhongli Mo, was invested with the Liang kingdom’s chancellor-of-state seal (Peng Yue’s office), and gained four thousand more households.

sentences.zh: 從擊項羽二歲，攻胡陵。

translations.literal: He followed in attacking Xiang Yu for two years and assaulted Huling.
translations.idiomatic: For two years he fought Xiang Yu’s forces and joined the attack on Huling.

sentences.zh: 漢王即帝位，燕王臧荼反，商以將軍從擊荼，戰龍脫，先登陷陣，破荼軍易下，卻敵，遷為右丞相，賜爵列侯，與剖符，世世勿絕，食邑涿郡五千戶。

translations.literal: When the King of Han assumed the imperial title, King Zang Tu of Yan rebelled; Shang as a general followed in striking Tu, fought at Longtuo, was first to ascend and pierce the line, destroyed Tu’s army below Yi, drove back the enemy, was promoted to right chancellor, was granted a full marquis’s rank with a tally-splitting patent perpetual through the generations, and fed at Zhuo command with five thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: After the King of Han took the throne, Zang Tu of Yan rose in revolt; Shang marched as a general, fought at Longtuo, led the escalade through the enemy ranks, shattered Tu’s host beneath Yi, was raised to right chancellor, ennobled as a full marquis with an everlasting split tally, and drew income from five thousand households in Zhuo command.

sentences.zh: 別定上谷，因攻代，受趙相國印。

translations.literal: Separately he settled Shanggu, then attacked Dai and received the seal of Zhao chancellor of state.
translations.idiomatic: He independently secured Shanggu, then turned on Dai and was invested as Zhao’s chancellor of state.

sentences.zh: 與絳侯等定代郡、鴈門，得代丞相程縱、守相郭同、將軍以下至六百石十九人。

translations.literal: Together with the Marquis of Zhou and others he settled Dai commandery and Yanmen, capturing Zhao’s chancellor Cheng Zong, assistant governor Guo Tong, and from generals down to rank of six hundred piculs nineteen men in all.
translations.idiomatic: With the Marquis of Zhou he reduced Dai and Yanmen, taking Zhao Chancellor Cheng Zong, Defender Guo Tong, and nineteen officers from general rank down to six hundred piculs.

sentences.zh: 還，以將軍將太上皇衛一歲。

translations.literal: Returning, as a general he commanded the Grand Supreme Emperor’s guard for one year.
translations.idiomatic: On his return he spent a year as general commanding the Grand Supreme Emperor’s household guard.

sentences.zh: 十月，以右丞相擊陳豨，殘東垣。

translations.literal: In the tenth month, as right chancellor he struck Chen Xi and ravaged Dongyuan.
translations.idiomatic: That tenth month he led the right chancellor’s column against Chen Xi and razed Dongyuan.

sentences.zh: 又從擊黥布，攻其前垣，陷兩陳，得以破布軍，更封為曲周侯，食邑五千一百戶，除前所食。

translations.literal: Again he followed in striking Qing Bu, attacked his forward rampart, pierced two battle lines, and thereby broke Bu’s army; he was re-enfeoffed as Marquis of Quzhou with five thousand one hundred households, superseding his earlier grants.
translations.idiomatic: He joined the campaign against Qing Bu, stormed the forward wall, tore two formations apart, and broke Bu’s host; he was transferred to the marquisate of Quzhou with five thousand one hundred households, replacing his previous fiefs.

sentences.zh: 凡別破軍三，降定郡六，縣七十三，得丞相、守相、大將軍各一人，小將軍二人，二千石以下至六百石十九人。

translations.literal: In all, on detached commands he destroyed three armies, reduced and settled six commanderies and seventy-three counties, captured one chancellor, one assistant governor, and one grand general each, two lesser generals, and nineteen officials from two thousand piculs down to six hundred piculs.
translations.idiomatic: Across independent operations he shattered three field armies, brought six commanderies and seventy-three counties to submission, and took one chancellor, one assistant governor, one grand general, two brigade generals, and nineteen officers between two thousand and six hundred piculs.

sentences.zh: 商事孝惠帝、呂后。

translations.literal: Shang served Emperor Hui and Empress Lü.
translations.idiomatic: Shang served under Emperor Hui and Empress Dowager Lü.

sentences.zh: 呂后崩，商疾不治事。

translations.literal: When Empress Lü died, Shang was ill and did not handle affairs.
translations.idiomatic: After Empress Lü’s death Shang lay sick and ceased to administer government.

sentences.zh: 其子寄，子況，與呂祿善。

translations.literal: His son Ji begot Kuang, and Ji was on good terms with Lü Lu.
translations.idiomatic: His son Ji, who had a son Kuang, was intimate with Lü Lu.

sentences.zh: 及高后崩，大臣欲誅諸呂，呂祿為將軍，軍於北軍，太尉勃不得入北軍，於是乃使人劫商，令其子寄紿呂祿。

translations.literal: When the Empress Dowager Gao died, the great ministers wished to execute the various Lüs; Lü Lu was general and encamped the army at the Northern Army, and the grand commandant Zhou could not enter the Northern Army; thereupon they sent men to seize Shang and ordered his son Ji to deceive Lü Lu.
translations.idiomatic: After the empress dowager’s death the ministers plotted to extirpate the Lü clan; Lü Lu held the Northern Army encampment and barred Zhou Bo’s entry, so they kidnapped Shang and forced his son Ji to lure Lü Lu out.

sentences.zh: 呂祿信之，與出游，而太尉勃乃得入據北軍，遂以誅諸呂。

translations.literal: Lü Lu trusted him, went out roaming with him, and the Grand Commandant Zhou was then able to enter and occupy the Northern Army, whereupon he executed the various Lüs.
translations.idiomatic: Lü Lu took the bait and rode out on pleasure with Ji, allowing Zhou Bo to slip in, seize the Northern Army, and wipe out the Lüs.

sentences.zh: 商是歲薨，諡曰景侯。

translations.literal: Shang died that year and was posthumously titled Accomplished Marquis.
translations.idiomatic: Shang died the same year with the posthumous name Accomplished Marquis.

sentences.zh: 子寄嗣。

translations.literal: His son Ji succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His heir was Ji.

sentences.zh: 天下稱酈況賣友。

translations.literal: All under Heaven said Li Kuang had sold his friend.
translations.idiomatic: People everywhere said Li Kuang had betrayed his friend.

sentences.zh: 孝景時，吳、楚、齊、趙反，上以寄為將軍，圍趙城，七月不能下。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Jing, Wu, Chu, Qi, and Zhao rebelled; the emperor appointed Ji as general to besiege the Zhao capital, but for seven months he could not take it.
translations.idiomatic: When Wu, Chu, Qi, and Zhao rose under Emperor Jing, the throne named Ji general to invest Zhao’s walls, yet seven months brought no breach.

sentences.zh: 欒布自平齊來，乃滅趙。

translations.literal: Luan Bu came from having pacified Qi and then extinguished Zhao.
translations.idiomatic: Only when Luan Bu arrived from the pacification of Qi could Zhao be crushed.

sentences.zh: 孝景中二年，寄欲取平原君姊為夫人，景帝怒，下寄吏，免。

translations.literal: In the second year of Emperor Jing’s middle era Ji wished to take the Elder Princess of Pingyuan’s elder sister as his principal wife; Emperor Jing was angry, handed Ji over to the judges, and dismissed him.
translations.idiomatic: Mid-reign of Emperor Jing, year two, Ji tried to wed the Lady of Pingyuan’s elder sister as his chief consort; the emperor, furious, jailed Ji and stripped his title.

sentences.zh: 上乃封商它子堅為繆侯，奉商後。

translations.literal: The emperor thereupon enfeoffed another son of Shang, Jian, as Marquis of Miu to maintain Shang’s posterity.
translations.idiomatic: He then created Shang’s other son Jian as Marquis of Miu to continue the line.

sentences.zh: 傳至玄孫終根，武帝時為太常，坐巫蠱誅，國除。

translations.literal: The title passed to the great-great-grandson Zhonggen, who under Emperor Wu was grand master of ceremonies and was executed for witchcraft intrigue; the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: It descended to great-great-grandson Zhonggen, who as grand master of ceremonies under Emperor Wu lost his life and fief to the witchcraft prosecutions.

sentences.zh: 元始中，賜高祖時功臣自酈商以下子孫爵乎關內侯，食邑凡百餘人。

translations.literal: In the Yuanshi era he granted descendants of the High Ancestor’s meritorious ministers from Li Shang downward noble rank of marquis-within-the-passes; those with food fiefs numbered more than a hundred.
translations.idiomatic: During Yuanshi the court revived honors for the High Ancestor’s old companions from Li Shang on down, ennobling more than a hundred of their heirs as marquises-within-the-passes.

sentences.zh: 夏侯嬰

translations.literal: Xiahou Ying
translations.idiomatic: Xiahou Ying

sentences.zh: 夏侯嬰，沛人也。

translations.literal: Xiahou Ying was a man of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: Xiahou Ying came from Pei.

sentences.zh: 為沛廄司御，每送使客，還過泗上亭，與高祖語，未嘗不移日也。

translations.literal: He was stable groom and charioteer for the Pei stable; whenever he escorted envoys and guests, returning he would pass the Sishang pavilion and converse with the High Ancestor, never failing to forget the day.
translations.idiomatic: He drove the county stable’s carriages; each time he convoyed guests past the Sishang pavilion he would linger talking with the High Ancestor until dusk.

sentences.zh: 嬰已而試補縣吏，與高祖相愛。

translations.literal: Afterward Ying was on trial appointment as a county clerk and was mutually fond with the High Ancestor.
translations.idiomatic: Later, on probation as a county clerk, he grew close to the High Ancestor.

sentences.zh: 高祖戲而傷嬰，人有告高祖。

translations.literal: The High Ancestor in jest injured Ying; someone reported the High Ancestor.
translations.idiomatic: The High Ancestor horseplayed and hurt Ying; an informer denounced him.

sentences.zh: 高祖時為亭長，重坐傷人，告故不傷嬰，嬰證之。

translations.literal: At the time the High Ancestor was pavilion chief; for the serious crime of wounding a man he reported that originally he had not injured Ying, and Ying testified to it.
translations.idiomatic: The High Ancestor was then pavilion chief; charged with grave assault, he swore he had never hurt Ying, and Ying backed his lie.

sentences.zh: 移獄覆，嬰坐高祖繫歲餘，掠笞數百，終脫高祖。

translations.literal: The case was transferred for review; Ying for the High Ancestor’s sake sat in bonds for more than a year, was flogged with the bamboo several hundred strokes, and in the end freed the High Ancestor.
translations.idiomatic: When the file was reopened Ying took the blame and spent over a year in fetters under the rod, yet still cleared the High Ancestor.

sentences.zh: 高祖之初與徒屬欲攻沛也，嬰時以縣令史為高祖使。

translations.literal: At the beginning when the High Ancestor with his followers wished to attack Pei, Ying at the time as county clerk scribe was the High Ancestor’s envoy.
translations.idiomatic: When the High Ancestor first gathered his band to strike Pei, Ying served as county clerk-scribe and rode as his messenger.

sentences.zh: 上降沛一日，高祖為沛公，賜爵七大夫，以嬰為太僕，常奉車。

translations.literal: On the day the governor submitted Pei, the High Ancestor became Lord of Pei; he granted the rank of seventh-order grandee and appointed Ying grand coachman, constantly handling the chariot.
translations.idiomatic: The day Pei’s magistrate capitulated, the High Ancestor was proclaimed Lord of Pei; he raised Ying to seventh-order grandee and named him grand coachman to drive the chariot.

sentences.zh: 從攻胡陵，嬰與蕭何降泗水監平，平以胡陵降，賜嬰爵五大夫。

translations.literal: He followed in attacking Huling; Ying together with Xiao He induced the Sishui intendant Ping to surrender; Ping through Huling surrendered, and Ying was granted the rank of fifth-order grandee.
translations.idiomatic: At Huling he and Xiao He persuaded Sishui Intendant Ping to yield; for that capitulation Ying rose to fifth-order grandee.

sentences.zh: 從擊秦軍碭東，攻濟陽，下戶牖，破李由軍雍丘，以兵車趣攻戰疾，破之，賜爵執帛。

translations.literal: He followed in striking Qin troops east of Dang, attacked Jiyang, took Huyou, destroyed Li You’s army at Yongqiu; because with war chariots he pressed attacks and fought swiftly, he defeated them and was granted the rank of grandee bearing silk.
translations.idiomatic: East of Dang he hit Qin forces, stormed Jiyang, seized Huyou, broke Li You at Yongqiu, and for dashing chariot assaults earned the rank of grandee bearing silk.

sentences.zh: 從擊章邯軍東阿、濮陽下，以兵車趣攻戰疾，破之，賜爵執圭。

translations.literal: He followed in striking Zhang Han’s army east of Dong’e and below Puyang; because with war chariots he pressed attacks and fought swiftly, he defeated them and was granted the rank of grandee bearing jade tablet.
translations.idiomatic: He fought Zhang Han at Dong’e and beneath Puyang, again driving chariot charges that won the rank of grandee bearing jade tablet.

sentences.zh: 從擊趙賁軍開封，楊熊軍曲遇。

translations.literal: He followed in striking Zhao Fen’s army at Kaifeng and Yang Xiong’s army at Quyu.
translations.idiomatic: He joined the battles against Zhao Fen at Kaifeng and Yang Xiong at Quyu.

sentences.zh: 嬰從捕虜六十八人，降卒八百五十人，得印一匱。

translations.literal: Ying followed in capturing sixty-eight prisoners, causing eight hundred fifty surrendered troops to yield, and obtained one chest of seals.
translations.idiomatic: In those actions Ying took sixty-eight captives, accepted eight hundred fifty capitulations, and seized a whole case of official seals.

sentences.zh: 又擊秦軍雒陽東，以兵車趣攻戰疾，賜爵封，轉為滕令。

translations.literal: Again he struck Qin troops east of Luoyang; because with war chariots he pressed attacks and fought swiftly, he was granted a noble fief and was transferred to be magistrate of Teng.
translations.idiomatic: East of Luoyang he shattered another Qin detachment with swift chariot work, earned a fief grant, and was reassigned as magistrate of Teng.

sentences.zh: 因奉車從攻定南陽，戰於藍田、芷陽，至霸上。

translations.literal: Thereupon handling the chariot he followed in attacking and settling Nanyang, fought at Lantian and Zhiyang, and reached Bashang.
translations.idiomatic: Still as charioteer he campaigned through Nanyang, fought at Lantian and Zhiyang, and drove to Bashang.

sentences.zh: 沛公為漢王，賜嬰爵列侯，號昭平侯，復為太僕，從入蜀漢。

translations.literal: Lord Pei became King of Han; he granted Ying a full marquis’s rank with the title Marquis of Zhaoping, again made him grand coachman, and he followed in entering Shu and Han.
translations.idiomatic: Made King of Han, Lord Pei ennobled Ying as Marquis of Zhaoping, restored him as grand coachman, and took him into Shu and Han.

sentences.zh: 還定三秦，從擊項籍。

translations.literal: On the return march that pacified the Three Qin, he followed in striking Xiang Ji.
translations.idiomatic: On the march back to settle the Three Qin he fought Xiang Ji.

sentences.zh: 至彭城，項羽大破漢軍。

translations.literal: Reaching Pengcheng, Xiang Yu heavily defeated the Han army.
translations.idiomatic: At Pengcheng Xiang Yu shattered the Han host.

sentences.zh: 漢王不利，馳去。

translations.literal: The King of Han was disadvantaged and galloped away.
translations.idiomatic: The King of Han broke and fled at full gallop.

sentences.zh: 見孝惠、魯元，載之。

translations.literal: Seeing Emperor Hui and Princess Lu Yuan, he loaded them on the chariot.
translations.idiomatic: He caught sight of Emperor Hui and the Princess of Lu and swept them into the carriage.

sentences.zh: 漢王急，馬罷，虜在後，常蹳兩兒棄之，嬰常收載行，面雍樹馳。

translations.literal: The King of Han was in straits, the horses were exhausted, pursuers were behind, and he often kicked the two children off wanting to abandon them; Ying always picked them up and carried them along, facing the horses and running with the canopy trees.
translations.idiomatic: With mounts blown and pursuers closing, the king repeatedly tried to pitch the children from the chariot; Ying kept snatching them back, turning the horses and shielding the youngsters with the carriage curtains.

sentences.zh: 漢王怒，欲斬嬰者十餘，卒得脫，而致孝惠、魯元於豐。

translations.literal: The King of Han was angry and more than ten times wished to behead Ying; in the end he got away free and delivered Emperor Hui and Princess Lu Yuan to Feng.
translations.idiomatic: The king drew his sword on Ying a dozen times, yet Ying brought him through and landed Emperor Hui and the princess safely at Feng.

sentences.zh: 漢王既至滎陽，收散兵，復振，賜嬰食邑沂陽。

translations.literal: When the King of Han reached Xingyang, he collected scattered troops, revived his strength, and granted Ying a food fief at Yiyang.
translations.idiomatic: At Xingyang he rallied broken units, found new heart, and assigned Ying income at Yiyang.

sentences.zh: 擊項籍下邑，追至陳，卒定楚。

translations.literal: He struck Xiang Ji at Xiayi, pursued to Chen, and in the end settled Chu.
translations.idiomatic: He hit Xiang Ji near Xiayi, chased him to Chen, and helped finish Chu.

sentences.zh: 至魯，益食茲氏。

translations.literal: Reaching Lu, his food income was increased at Zishi.
translations.idiomatic: Reaching Lu he had Zishi added to his grant.

sentences.zh: 漢王即帝位，燕王臧荼反，嬰從擊荼。

translations.literal: When the King of Han assumed the imperial title, King Zang Tu of Yan rebelled, and Ying followed in striking Tu.
translations.idiomatic: When the throne was taken, Zang Tu of Yan revolted and Ying marched against him.

sentences.zh: 明年，從至陳，取楚王信。

translations.literal: The next year he followed to Chen and seized the King of Chu Xin.
translations.idiomatic: The following year he went to Chen and helped arrest King Xin of Chu.

sentences.zh: 更食汝陰，剖符，世世勿絕。

translations.literal: His food income was changed to Ruoyin with tally-splitting, perpetual through the generations.
translations.idiomatic: His fief was shifted to Ruoyin with a perpetual split tally.

sentences.zh: 從擊代，至武泉、雲中，益食千戶。

translations.literal: He followed in striking Dai, reaching Wuquan and Yunzhong, and his food income was increased by one thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: He campaigned in Dai as far as Wuquan and Yunzhong and gained another thousand households.

sentences.zh: 因從擊韓信軍胡騎晉陽旁，大破之。

translations.literal: Thereupon he followed in striking Han Xin’s Hu cavalry beside Jinyang and heavily defeated them.
translations.idiomatic: He then rode against Han Xin’s Hu horse near Jinyang and routed them.

sentences.zh: 追北至平城，為胡所圍，七日不得通。

translations.literal: Pursuing north to Pingcheng he was surrounded by the Hu and for seven days could not get through.
translations.idiomatic: He chased north to Pingcheng, where the Hu ringed him for seven days without a gap.

sentences.zh: 高帝使使厚遺閼氏，冒頓乃開其圍一角。

translations.literal: The High Emperor sent envoys with rich gifts to the Yan Zhi lady, and Modun then opened one corner of his encirclement.
translations.idiomatic: The High Emperor bribed Modun’s chief wife, the Yan Zhi, and Modun relaxed one sector of the ring.

sentences.zh: 高帝出欲馳，嬰固徐行，弩皆持滿外鄉，卒以得脫。

translations.literal: The High Emperor wished to gallop out; Ying firmly made him go slowly, crossbows all drawn full and facing outward, and in the end they got away free.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor wanted to bolt; Ying insisted on a slow withdrawal with every crossbow cocked outward, and so they slipped the trap.

sentences.zh: 益食嬰細陽千戶。

translations.literal: Ying’s food income was increased by one thousand households at Xiyang.
translations.idiomatic: Another thousand households at Xiyang were added to Ying’s fief.

sentences.zh: 從擊胡騎句注北，大破之。

translations.literal: He followed in striking Hu cavalry north of Gouzhu and heavily defeated them.
translations.idiomatic: North of Gouzhu he smashed Hu cavalry again.

sentences.zh: 擊胡騎平城南，三陷陳，功為多，閼所奪邑五百戶。

translations.literal: He struck Hu cavalry south of Pingcheng, three times pierced formations; merit was much, and five hundred households of fief were taken away by the consort.
translations.idiomatic: South of Pingcheng he drove Hu horse three times through the enemy ranks; his merit was outstanding, yet the imperial consort’s faction stripped five hundred households from his fief.

sentences.zh: 從擊陳豨、黥布軍，陷陳卻敵，益千戶，定食汝陰六千九百戶，除前所食。

translations.literal: He followed in striking the armies of Chen Xi and Qing Bu, pierced formations and drove back the enemy; his income was increased by one thousand households; his settled food fief at Ruyin was six thousand nine hundred households, superseding his earlier grants.
translations.idiomatic: Against Chen Xi and Qing Bu he tore enemy lines and threw them back, added a thousand households, and fixed his permanent grant at Ruyin at six thousand nine hundred, superseding earlier assignments.

sentences.zh: 嬰自上初起沛，常為太僕從，竟高祖崩。

translations.literal: Ying from the High Ancestor’s first rising at Pei constantly was grand coachman following him, until the High Ancestor’s death.
translations.idiomatic: From the first uprising at Pei until the High Ancestor’s death Ying never left the post of grand coachman.

sentences.zh: 以太僕事惠帝。

translations.literal: As grand coachman he served Emperor Hui.
translations.idiomatic: He continued as grand coachman under Emperor Hui.

sentences.zh: 惠帝及高后德嬰之脫孝惠、魯元於下邑間也，乃賜嬰北第第一，曰「

translations.literal: Emperor Hui and the Empress Gao were grateful that Ying had freed Emperor Hui and Princess Lu Yuan between at Xiayi, and therefore granted Ying the foremost northern mansion, saying “
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Hui and Empress Lü remembered how Ying had saved the heir and the Princess of Lu during the flight from Xiayi, and gave him the premier mansion north of the palace, saying, “

sentences.zh: 近我」，以尊異之。

translations.literal: Be near me,” thereby honoring him exceptionally.
translations.idiomatic: Stay close to us,” to mark him apart from other ministers.

sentences.zh: 惠帝崩，以太僕事高后。

translations.literal: When Emperor Hui died, as grand coachman he served the Empress Gao.
translations.idiomatic: After Emperor Hui’s death he served Empress Dowager Lü still as grand coachman.

sentences.zh: 高后崩，代王之來，嬰以太僕與東牟侯入清宮，廢少帝，以天子法駕迎代王代邸，與大臣共立文帝，復為太僕。

translations.literal: When the Empress Gao died, at the coming of the King of Dai, Ying as grand coachman together with the Marquis of Dongmou entered to purge the palace, deposed the Young Emperor, with the imperial chariot train welcomed the King of Dai from the King of Dai’s lodge at court, and together with the great ministers established Emperor Wen, again becoming grand coachman.
translations.idiomatic: When she died and the King of Dai approached the capital, Ying rode with the Marquis of Dongmou into the palace to cleanse it, set aside the boy emperor, met the King of Dai with full imperial equipage at his city residence, helped enthrone Emperor Wen, and resumed the office of grand coachman.

sentences.zh: 八歲薨，諡曰文侯。

translations.literal: After eight years he died and was posthumously titled Cultured Marquis.
translations.idiomatic: Eight years into Emperor Wen’s reign he died, receiving the posthumous epithet Cultured Marquis.

sentences.zh: 傳至曾孫頗，尚平陽公主，坐與父御婢奸，自殺，國除。

translations.literal: The title passed to the great-grandson Po, who married the Princess of Pingyang; for committing adultery with his father’s chariot maidservant he committed suicide, and the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: It came down to great-grandson Po, who wed the Princess of Pingyang but killed himself after bedding his father’s carriage maid, and the fief lapsed.

sentences.zh: 初嬰為滕令奉車，故號滕公。

translations.literal: Earlier Ying as magistrate of Teng handled the chariot, therefore he was titled Lord of Teng.
translations.idiomatic: Because he had been Teng magistrate while driving the royal chariot, men called him Lord of Teng.

sentences.zh: 及曾孫頗尚主，主隨外家姓，號孫公主，故滕公子孫更為孫氏。

translations.literal: When the great-grandson Po married the princess, the princess took her maternal clan’s surname and was titled Princess Sun, therefore the descendants of the Lord of Teng changed their surname to Sun.
translations.idiomatic: When Po married the princess she adopted her mother’s Sun surname as Princess Sun, so Ying’s line thereafter bore the name Sun.

sentences.zh: 灌嬰，睢陽販繒者也。

translations.literal: Guan Ying was a man of Suiyang who dealt in silk by trade.
translations.idiomatic: Guan Ying was a Suiyang silk merchant.

sentences.zh: 高祖為沛公，略地至雍丘，章邯殺項梁，而沛公還軍於碭，嬰以中涓從，擊破東郡尉於成武及秦軍於杠里，疾鬥，賜爵七大夫。

translations.literal: When the High Ancestor was Lord of Pei and overran the land reaching Yongqiu, Zhang Han killed Xiang Liang, and Lord Pei returned his army to Dang; Ying as palace attendant followed, defeated the Dongjun assistant at Chengwu and Qin troops at Gangli, fought fiercely, and was granted the rank of seventh-order grandee.
translations.idiomatic: Lord Pei was reducing territory to Yongqiu when Zhang Han slew Xiang Liang and pulled back to Dang; Ying followed as palace attendant, broke the Dongjun assistant at Chengwu and Qin columns at Gangli in sharp fighting, and won seventh-order grandee.

sentences.zh: 又從攻秦軍亳南、開封、曲遇，戰疾力，賜爵執帛，號宣陵君。

translations.literal: Again he followed in attacking Qin troops south of Bo, at Kaifeng, and at Quyu, fighting with fierce exertion, and was granted the rank of grandee bearing silk with the title Lord of Xuanling.
translations.idiomatic: He fought again south of Bo, at Kaifeng, and at Quyu with furious energy, earning silk grandee rank as Lord of Xuanling.

sentences.zh: 從攻陽武以西至雒陽，破秦軍尸北。

translations.literal: He followed in attacking from Yangwu westward to Luoyang, destroyed Qin troops north of Shi,
translations.idiomatic: From Yangwu west to Luoyang he shattered Qin forces north of Shi,

sentences.zh: 北絕河津，南破南陽守齮陽城東，遂定南陽郡。

translations.literal: north cut the ferry on the Yellow River, south defeated Nanyang governor Qi east of Yangcheng, and thereupon settled Nanyang commandery.
translations.idiomatic: severed the northern river crossing, then south routed Nanyang Governor Qi east of Yangcheng and secured the whole commandery.

sentences.zh: 西入武關，戰於藍田，疾力，至霸上，賜爵執圭，號昌文君。

translations.literal: Westward he entered Wuguan, fought at Lantian with fierce exertion, reached Bashang, was granted the rank of grandee bearing jade tablet, and was titled Lord of Changwen.
translations.idiomatic: He pushed through Wuguan, bloodied Lantian in a hard fight, reached Bashang, and was named jade-tablet grandee Lord of Changwen.

sentences.zh: 沛公為漢王，拜嬰為郎中，從入漢中，十月，拜為中謁者。

translations.literal: Lord Pei became King of Han; he appointed Ying gentleman-of-the-palace, and he followed in entering Hanzhong; in the tenth month he was appointed palace usher.
translations.idiomatic: Made King of Han, Lord Pei made Ying gentleman-of-the-palace for the march into Hanzhong, then in the tenth month promoted him to palace usher.

sentences.zh: 從還定三秦，下櫟陽，降塞王。

translations.literal: He followed on the return that settled the Three Qin, took Liyang, and caused the King of Sai to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: On the drive to pacify the Three Qin he seized Liyang and forced the King of Sai to capitulate.

sentences.zh: 還圍章邯廢丘，未拔。

translations.literal: He returned to besiege Zhang Han at Feiqiu but did not yet take it.
translations.idiomatic: He helped invest Zhang Han at Feiqiu without yet storming the town.

sentences.zh: 從東出臨晉關，擊降殷王，定其地。

translations.literal: He followed east out of Linjin Pass, struck and caused the King of Yin to surrender, and settled his territory.
translations.idiomatic: Marching east through Linjin he broke the King of Yin and annexed his lands.

sentences.zh: 擊項羽將龍且、魏相項佗軍定陶南，疾戰，破之。

translations.literal: He struck the generals of Xiang Yu Long Ju and Wei chancellor Xiang Tuo south of Dingtao, fought fiercely, and defeated them.
translations.idiomatic: South of Dingtao he defeated Long Ju and Wei Chancellor Xiang Tuo in a sharp engagement.

sentences.zh: 賜嬰爵列侯，號昌文侯，食杜平鄉。

translations.literal: Ying was granted a full marquis’s rank with the title Lord of Changwen, feeding at Duping village.
translations.idiomatic: The king ennobled Ying as full marquis with the style Lord of Changwen, his income drawn from Duping hamlet in Du.

sentences.zh: 復以中謁者從降下碭，以北至彭城。

translations.literal: Again as palace usher he followed in reducing and taking Dang, and northward to Pengcheng.
translations.idiomatic: Again as usher he helped reduce Dang and rode north to Pengcheng.

sentences.zh: 項羽擊破漢王，漢王遁而西，嬰從還，軍於雍丘。

translations.literal: Xiang Yu defeated the King of Han; the King of Han fled westward, and Ying followed in returning and encamped the army at Yongqiu.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiang Yu broke the Han king, the king bolted west; Ying fell back with him and drew up at Yongqiu.

sentences.zh: 王武、魏公申徒反，從擊破之。

translations.literal: Wang Wu and Duke Shen Tu of Wei rebelled; he followed in defeating them.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Wu and Duke Shentu of Wei rose; Ying joined the force that crushed them.

sentences.zh: 攻下外黃，西收軍於滎陽。

translations.literal: He attacked and took Waihuang, westward gathered troops at Xingyang.
translations.idiomatic: He stormed Waihuang, then rallied the army west at Xingyang.

sentences.zh: 楚騎來眾，漢王乃擇軍中可為騎將者，皆推故秦騎士重泉人李必、駱甲習騎兵，今為校尉，可為騎將。

translations.literal: Chu cavalry came in great numbers; the King of Han then selected from the army those who could be cavalry generals; all pushed forward the former Qin cavalrymen Li Bi and Luo Jia of Zhongquan, skilled in cavalry, now colonels, who could be cavalry generals.
translations.idiomatic: Chu horse swelled in strength, so the Han king looked for cavalry commanders; the men nominated former Qin troopers Li Bi and Luo Jia of Zhongquan, already colonels and masters of mounted warfare.

sentences.zh: 漢王欲拜之，必、甲曰：「臣故秦民，恐軍不信臣，臣願得大王左右善騎者傅之。」

translations.literal: The King of Han wished to appoint them; Bi and Jia said: “Your servants are former Qin people; we fear the army will not trust your servants; we wish to obtain from the Great King’s attendants those skilled in riding to instruct us.”
translations.idiomatic: The king was about to commission them, but they protested that as old Qin men they lacked the army’s confidence and asked a royal favorite who excelled at riding to serve as nominal commander.

sentences.zh: 嬰雖少，然數力戰，乃拜嬰為中大夫，令李必、駱甲為左右校尉，將郎中騎兵擊楚騎於滎陽東，大破之。

translations.literal: Ying though young had repeatedly fought with strength; thereupon he was appointed grandee of the palace, with Li Bi and Luo Jia as left and right colonels, leading gentlemen-of-the-palace cavalry to strike Chu cavalry east of Xingyang, heavily defeating them.
translations.idiomatic: Though still young Ying had proven himself in battle, so he was named grandee of the palace while Li Bi and Luo Jia became his left and right colonels; with the household cavalry he routed Chu horse east of Xingyang.

sentences.zh: 受詔別擊楚軍後，絕其饟道，起陽武至襄邑。

translations.literal: Receiving orders as a detached commander he struck Chu’s rear, cut their supply road from Yangwu to Xiangyi.
translations.idiomatic: Ordered to a separate column he fell on Chu’s rear and severed their convoy line from Yangwu to Xiangyi.

sentences.zh: 擊項羽之將項冠於魯下，破之，所將卒斬右司馬、騎將各一人。

translations.literal: He struck Xiang Yu’s general Xiang Guan below Lu, defeated him, and the troops he led beheaded one right marshal and one cavalry general each.
translations.idiomatic: Below Lu he smashed Xiang Guan and his men took a right marshal and a cavalry general.

sentences.zh: 擊破柘公王武軍燕西，所將卒斬樓煩將五人，連尹一人。

translations.literal: He destroyed Duke Zhe of Yan Wang Wu’s army west of Yan, and the troops he led beheaded five Loufan generals and one lian yin.
translations.idiomatic: West of Yan he broke Duke Zhe’s detachment of Wang Wu, claiming five Loufan generals and a lian yin.

sentences.zh: 擊王武別將桓嬰白馬下，破之，所將卒斬都尉一人。

translations.literal: He struck Wang Wu’s detached general Huan Ying below Baima, defeated him, and the troops he led beheaded one commandant-in-chief.
translations.idiomatic: At Baima he crushed Huan Ying, Wang Wu’s lieutenant, and slew a commandant-in-chief.

sentences.zh: 以騎度河南，送漢王到雒陽，從北迎相國韓信軍於邯鄲。

translations.literal: With cavalry he crossed south of the Yellow River, escorted the King of Han to Luoyang, and from the north welcomed Chancellor Han Xin’s army at Handan.
translations.idiomatic: He forded the river with horse, convoyed the king to Luoyang, then rode north to join Han Xin’s host at Handan.

sentences.zh: 還至敖倉，嬰遷為御史大夫。

translations.literal: Returning to Aocang, Ying was promoted to imperial counselor.
translations.idiomatic: Back at Aocang he was raised to imperial counselor.

sentences.zh: 三年，以列侯食邑杜平鄉。

translations.literal: In the third year, as a full marquis he fed at Duping village.
translations.idiomatic: In the third year of Han he drew his income as a columned marquis from Duping hamlet in Du.

sentences.zh: 受詔將郎中騎兵東屬相國韓信，擊破齊軍於歷下，所將卒虜單騎將軍華毋傷及將吏四十六人。

translations.literal: Receiving orders he led gentlemen-of-the-palace cavalry east to attach to Chancellor of State Han Xin, destroyed Qi troops below Lixia, and the soldiers he led captured lone-horse general Hua Wushang and forty-six officers.
translations.idiomatic: Ordered to bring the household horse east under Han Xin, he shattered the Qi army at Lixia, taking Hua Wushang—Qi’s “lone-horse” general—and forty-six of his staff.

sentences.zh: 降下臨淄，得相田光。

translations.literal: He caused Linzi to surrender and obtained Chancellor Tian Guang.
translations.idiomatic: Linzi capitulated to him, and he seized Qi’s chancellor Tian Guang.

sentences.zh: 追齊相田橫至嬴、博，擊破其騎，所將卒斬騎將一人，生得騎將四人。

translations.literal: He pursued Qi chancellor Tian Heng to Ying and Bo, destroyed his cavalry, and the soldiers he led beheaded one cavalry general and alive captured four cavalry generals.
translations.idiomatic: He chased Qi Chancellor Tian Heng to Ying and Bo, broke his mounted arm, slew one horse general, and took four others alive.

sentences.zh: 攻下嬴、博，破齊將軍田吸於千乘，斬之。

translations.literal: He attacked and took Ying and Bo, destroyed Qi general Tian Xi at Qiancheng, and beheaded him.
translations.idiomatic: He stormed Ying and Bo, crushed Qi general Tian Xi at Qiancheng, and struck off his head.

sentences.zh: 東從韓信攻龍且、留公於假密，卒斬龍且，生得右司馬、連尹各一人，樓煩將十人，身生得亞將周蘭。

translations.literal: Eastward following Han Xin he attacked Long Ju and Lord Liu at Gaomi, in the end beheaded Long Ju, alive captured one right marshal and one lian yin each, ten Loufan generals, and personally alive captured lieutenant general Zhou Lan.
translations.idiomatic: With Han Xin he struck Long Ju and Lord Liu at Gaomi, cut down Long Ju, bagged a right marshal and a lian yin, ten Loufan commanders, and with his own hands seized lieutenant general Zhou Lan.

sentences.zh: 齊地已定，韓信自立為齊王，使嬰別將擊楚將公杲於魯北，破之。

translations.literal: When Qi territory was settled, Han Xin established himself as King of Qi and ordered Ying as a detached commander to strike Chu general Gong Gao north of Lu and destroy him.
translations.idiomatic: After Qi submitted, Han Xin declared himself king of the land and detached Ying to smash Chu’s Gong Gao north of Lu.

sentences.zh: 轉南，破薛郡長，身虜騎將入。

translations.literal: Turning south he destroyed the Xue commandery chief and personally captured one cavalry general.
translations.idiomatic: Wheeling south he routed the Xue prefect and with his own hands seized an enemy horse commander.

sentences.zh: 攻博陽，前至下相以東南僮、取慮、徐。

translations.literal: He attacked Boyang, advanced to Xiapi and east through Nan tong, Qu lu, and Xu.
translations.idiomatic: He hit Boyang, then swept east from Xiapi through Nan tong, Qu lu, and Xu.

sentences.zh: 度淮，盡降其城邑，至廣陵。

translations.literal: He crossed the Huai and wholly caused the cities and towns to surrender, reaching Guangling.
translations.idiomatic: He crossed the Huai, brought every town along the bank to heel, and pushed on to Guangling.

sentences.zh: 項羽使項聲、薛公、郯公復定淮北，嬰度淮擊破項聲、郯公下邳，斬薛公，下下邳、壽春。

translations.literal: Xiang Yu sent Xiang Sheng, the duke of Xue, and the duke of Tan again to settle the north of the Huai; Ying crossed the Huai, struck and destroyed Xiang Sheng and the duke of Tan at Xiapi, beheaded the duke of Xue, and took Xiapi and Shouchun.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang Yu dispatched Xiang Sheng and the lords of Xue and Tan to recover the Huai north; Ying forded the river, broke Xiang Sheng and the lord of Tan beneath Xiapi, slew the lord of Xue, and seized Xiapi and Shouchun.

sentences.zh: 擊破楚騎平陽，遂降彭城。

translations.literal: He destroyed Chu cavalry at Pingyang and thereupon caused Pengcheng to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: He routed Chu horse at Pingyang and forced Pengcheng to open its gates.

sentences.zh: 虜柱國項佗，降留、薛、沛、酇、蕭、相。

translations.literal: He captured pillar-of-state Xiang Tuo and caused Liu, Xue, Pei, Zan, Xiao, and Xiang to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: He took the Chu pillar-of-state Xiang Tuo and received the surrender of Liu, Xue, Pei, Zan, Xiao, and Xiang.

sentences.zh: 攻苦、譙，復得亞將。

translations.literal: He attacked Ku and Qiao and again obtained a lieutenant general.
translations.idiomatic: He struck Ku and Qiao and captured another lieutenant general.

sentences.zh: 與漢王會頤鄉。

translations.literal: He joined the King of Han at Yixiang.
translations.idiomatic: He linked up with the King of Han at Yixiang.

sentences.zh: 從擊項籍軍陳下，破之。

translations.literal: He followed in striking Xiang Ji’s army below Chen and destroyed it.
translations.idiomatic: He helped smash Xiang Ji’s host beneath Chen.

sentences.zh: 所將卒斬樓煩將二人，虜將八人。

translations.literal: The soldiers he led beheaded two Loufan generals and captured eight generals.
translations.idiomatic: His men cut down two Loufan commanders and seized eight enemy generals.

sentences.zh: 賜益食邑二千五百戶。

translations.literal: He was granted an increase of food fief of two thousand five hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: The court added two thousand five hundred households to his grant.

sentences.zh: 項籍敗垓下去也，嬰以御史大夫將車騎別追項籍至東城，破之。

translations.literal: When Xiang Ji was defeated and went away from Gaixia, Ying as imperial counselor led chariots and horse on a separate pursuit of Xiang Ji to Dongcheng and destroyed him.
translations.idiomatic: When Xiang Ji broke from Gaixia, Ying, still imperial counselor, took chariotry and cavalry on an independent chase to Dongcheng and broke him there.

sentences.zh: 所將卒五人共斬項籍，皆賜爵列侯。

translations.literal: Five soldiers he led together beheaded Xiang Ji; all were granted full marquis rank.
translations.idiomatic: Five of his troopers together claimed Xiang Ji’s head; each was ennobled as a full marquis.

sentences.zh: 降左右司馬各一人，卒萬二千人，盡得其軍將吏。

translations.literal: He caused the left marshal and the right marshal to surrender, twelve thousand common soldiers, and wholly obtained that army’s generals and officers.
translations.idiomatic: He accepted the capitulation of Chu’s left and right marshals, twelve thousand rank and file, and the entire roster of generals and staff.

sentences.zh: 下東城、歷陽。

translations.literal: He took Dongcheng and Liyang.
translations.idiomatic: Dongcheng and Liyang fell to him.

sentences.zh: 度江，破吳郡長吳下，得吳守，遂定吳、豫章、會稽郡。

translations.literal: He crossed the Yangtze, destroyed the Wu commandery chief below Wu, obtained the Wu governor, and thereupon settled Wu, Yuzhang, and Kuaiji commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: He crossed the river, shattered the Wu prefect beneath the walls, seized the governor, and brought Wu, Yuzhang, and Kuaiji to submission.

sentences.zh: 還定淮北，凡五十二縣。

translations.literal: Returning he settled the north of the Huai, in all fifty-two counties.
translations.idiomatic: On the return sweep he pacified fifty-two counties north of the Huai.

sentences.zh: 漢王即帝位，賜益嬰邑三千戶。

translations.literal: When the King of Han assumed the imperial title, he granted Ying an increase of three thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: At the enthronement the new emperor added three thousand households to Ying’s fief.

sentences.zh: 以車騎將軍從擊燕王荼。

translations.literal: As chariot-and-cavalry general he followed in striking King Tu of Yan.
translations.idiomatic: As chariot-and-cavalry general he marched against King Zang Tu of Yan.

sentences.zh: 明年，從至陳，取楚王信。

translations.literal: The next year he followed to Chen and seized the King of Chu Xin.
translations.idiomatic: The following year he went to Chen and helped arrest King Xin of Chu.

sentences.zh: 還，剖符，世世勿絕，食潁陰二千五百戶。

translations.literal: Returning, tally-splitting perpetual through the generations, he fed at Yingyin two thousand five hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: He came back with a perpetual split tally and fixed income of two thousand five hundred households at Yingyin.

sentences.zh: 從擊漢王信於代，至馬邑，別降樓煩以北六縣，斬代左將，破胡騎將於武泉北。

translations.literal: He followed in striking the King of Han Xin at Dai, reaching Mayi; separately he caused six counties north of Loufan to surrender, beheaded Dai’s left general, and destroyed a Hu cavalry general north of Wuquan.
translations.idiomatic: He campaigned against Han Xin of Dai as far as Mayi, detached a column that pacified six counties north of Loufan, slew Dai’s left general, and broke a Hu horse commander north of Wuquan.

sentences.zh: 復從擊信胡騎晉陽下，所將卒斬胡白題將一人。

translations.literal: Again he followed in striking Xin’s Hu cavalry below Jinyang; the soldiers he led beheaded one Hu Bai-di general.
translations.idiomatic: Again below Jinyang he hit Han Xin’s Hu riders, his men taking the head of a Bai-di commander.

sentences.zh: 又受詔并將燕、趙、齊、梁、楚車騎，擊破胡騎於硰石。

translations.literal: Again receiving orders he jointly commanded Yan, Zhao, Qi, Liang, and Chu chariots and horse and destroyed Hu cavalry at Shashi.
translations.idiomatic: He was then ordered to combine the chariotry and horse of Yan, Zhao, Qi, Liang, and Chu and shatter Hu cavalry at Shashi.

sentences.zh: 至平城，為胡所困。

translations.literal: Reaching Pingcheng he was trapped by the Hu.
translations.idiomatic: At Pingcheng the Hu hemmed him in.

sentences.zh: 從擊陳豨，別攻豨丞相侯敞軍曲逆下，破之，卒斬敞及特將五人。

translations.literal: He followed in striking Chen Xi; separately he attacked Chen Xi’s chancellor Hou Chang’s army below Quni, destroyed it, and in the end beheaded Chang and five specially appointed generals.
translations.idiomatic: Against Chen Xi he led a separate thrust at Chancellor Hou Chang below Quni, wiped out the column, and slew Chang plus five picked generals.

sentences.zh: 降曲逆、盧奴、上曲陽、安國、安平。

translations.literal: He caused Quni, Lunu, Shangquyang, Anguo, and Anping to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: Quni, Lunu, Shangquyang, Anguo, and Anping capitulated in turn.

sentences.zh: 攻下東垣。

translations.literal: He attacked and took Dongyuan.
translations.idiomatic: He stormed Dongyuan.

sentences.zh: 黥布反，以車騎將軍先出，攻布別將於相，破之，斬亞將樓煩將三人。

translations.literal: When Qing Bu rebelled, as chariot-and-cavalry general he went out first, attacked Bu’s detached general at Xiang, destroyed him, and beheaded three lieutenant generals and Loufan generals.
translations.idiomatic: Qing Bu’s revolt saw Ying, as chariot-and-cavalry general, move first: he smashed a lieutenant of Bu’s at Xiang and took three deputy and Loufan commanders’ heads.

sentences.zh: 又進擊破布上柱國及大司馬軍。

translations.literal: Again advancing he struck and destroyed Bu’s supreme pillar-of-state and grand marshal’s army.
translations.idiomatic: Pressing on he broke the host of Bu’s supreme pillar-of-state and grand marshal.

sentences.zh: 又進破布別將肥銖。

translations.literal: Again advancing he destroyed Bu’s detached general Fei Zhu.
translations.idiomatic: Another push crushed Bu’s lieutenant Fei Zhu.

sentences.zh: 嬰身生得左司馬一人，所將卒斬其小將十人，追北至淮上。

translations.literal: Ying personally alive captured one left marshal; the soldiers he led beheaded his lesser generals ten men; pursuing north they reached the Huai.
translations.idiomatic: Ying himself seized a left marshal alive; his men struck off ten subordinate generals and chased the fugitives to the Huai.

sentences.zh: 益食邑二千五百戶。

translations.literal: His food fief was increased by two thousand five hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: Another twenty-five hundred households were added to his estate.

sentences.zh: 布已破，高帝歸，定令嬰食潁陰五千戶，除前所食邑。

translations.literal: When Bu was already destroyed, the High Emperor returned and ordered Ying to feed at Yingyin five thousand households, rescinding his previous food fiefs.
translations.idiomatic: After Bu fell the High Emperor fixed Ying’s income at five thousand households in Yingyin, canceling earlier assignments.

sentences.zh: 凡從所得二千石二人，別破軍十六，降城四十六，定國一，郡二，縣五十二，得將軍二人，柱國、相各一人，二千石十人。

translations.literal: In all on campaigns where he followed he obtained two men of two thousand piculs; on separate commands he destroyed sixteen armies, caused forty-six cities to surrender, settled one kingdom, two commanderies, and fifty-two counties, obtained two generals, one pillar-of-state and one chancellor each, and ten men of two thousand piculs.
translations.idiomatic: Summing his service: he captured two two-thousand-picul officers in the main host, on his own broke sixteen armies, took forty-six towns, secured one kingdom, two commanderies, and fifty-two counties, and brought in two generals, a pillar-of-state, a chancellor, and ten officers at two thousand piculs.

sentences.zh: 嬰自破布歸，高帝崩，以列侯事惠帝及呂后。

translations.literal: From the time Ying returned after destroying Bu until the High Emperor’s death, as a full marquis he served Emperor Hui and Empress Lü.
translations.idiomatic: Between his return from the Bu campaign and the High Emperor’s death he served Emperor Hui and Empress Lü as a columned marquis.

sentences.zh: 呂后崩，呂祿等欲為亂。

translations.literal: When Empress Lü died, Lü Lu and others wished to make rebellion.
translations.idiomatic: After Empress Lü’s death Lü Lu and his kin plotted revolt.

sentences.zh: 齊哀王聞之，舉兵西，呂祿等以嬰為大將軍往擊之。

translations.literal: When King Ai of Qi heard it, he raised troops westward; Lü Lu and others appointed Ying grand marshal to go strike him.
translations.idiomatic: King Ai of Qi marched west at the news; the Lüs named Ying grand marshal to meet him.

sentences.zh: 嬰至滎陽，乃與絳侯等謀，因屯兵滎陽，風齊王以誅呂氏事，齊兵止不前。

translations.literal: When Ying reached Xingyang, then with the Marquis of Zhou and others he plotted; thereupon he encamped troops at Xingyang and sent word to the King of Qi about executing the Lü clan; Qi’s army halted and did not advance.
translations.idiomatic: At Xingyang Ying met Zhou Bo and the rest, held the army there, and tipped off the Qi king about the plan to extirpate the Lüs; the Qi host stopped in its tracks.

sentences.zh: 絳侯等既誅諸呂，齊王罷兵歸。

translations.literal: When the Marquis of Zhou and others had already executed the various Lüs, the King of Qi dismissed troops and returned.
translations.idiomatic: Once Zhou Bo had finished the purge, the king of Qi stood down and went home.

sentences.zh: 嬰自滎陽還，與絳侯、陳平共立文帝。

translations.literal: Ying returned from Xingyang and together with the Marquis of Zhou and Chen Ping jointly established Emperor Wen.
translations.idiomatic: Ying rode back from Xingyang with Zhou Bo and Chen Ping to enthrone Emperor Wen.

sentences.zh: 於是益封嬰三千戶，賜金千斤，為太尉。
