translations.literal: Thereupon Ying’s fief was increased by three thousand households, he was granted a thousand jin of gold, and was made grand commandant.
translations.idiomatic: He was then given three thousand more households, a thousand jin of gold, and the post of grand commandant.

sentences.zh: 三歲，絳侯勃免相，嬰為丞相，罷太尉官。

translations.literal: At three years the Marquis of Zhou Bo was dismissed as chancellor; Ying became chancellor and the office of grand commandant was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Three years later Zhou Bo left the chancellorship; Ying took it while the grand commandant’s post was left vacant.

sentences.zh: 是歲，匈奴大入北地，上令丞相嬰將騎八萬五千擊匈奴。

translations.literal: That year the Xiongnu massively entered Beidi; the emperor ordered Chancellor Ying to lead eighty-five thousand horse to strike the Xiongnu.
translations.idiomatic: The same year the Xiongnu poured into Beidi; the emperor sent Chancellor Ying at the head of eighty-five thousand cavalry against them.

sentences.zh: 匈奴去，濟北王反，詔罷嬰兵。

translations.literal: The Xiongnu departed; the king of Jibei rebelled, and an edict dismissed Ying’s troops.
translations.idiomatic: When the nomads withdrew, the king of Jibei rose; an edict recalled Ying’s army.

sentences.zh: 後歲餘，以丞相薨，諡曰懿侯。

translations.literal: After more than a year, as chancellor he died and was posthumously titled Marquis Yi.
translations.idiomatic: A year and more later he died in office as chancellor, posthumously named Marquis Yi.

sentences.zh: 傳至孫疆，有罪，絕。

translations.literal: The title passed to the grandson Qiang, who for crime was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: The line reached grandson Qiang, who lost the fief to a conviction.

sentences.zh: 武帝復封嬰孫賢為臨汝侯，奉嬰後，後有罪，國除。

translations.literal: Emperor Wu again enfeoffed Ying’s grandson Xian as Marquis of Linru to maintain Ying’s posterity; later for crime the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu restored the house by making Ying’s grandson Xian Marquis of Linru; that line too fell to a crime and the fief lapsed.

sentences.zh: 傅寬，以魏五大夫騎將從，為舍人，起橫陽。

translations.literal: Fu Kuan, as Wei fifth-order grandee cavalry general follower, was a house-guest, rising from Hengyang.
translations.idiomatic: Fu Kuan began as a Wei fifth-order grandee and horse commander, serving as a house-guest from the uprising at Hengyang.

sentences.zh: 從攻安陽、杠里，趙賁軍於開封，及擊楊熊曲遇、陽武，斬首十二級，賜爵卿。

translations.literal: He followed in attacking Anyang and Gangli, Zhao Fen’s army at Kaifeng, and in striking Yang Xiong at Quyu and Yangwu, beheading twelve heads, and was granted ministerial grandee rank.
translations.idiomatic: He fought at Anyang and Gangli, at Kaifeng against Zhao Fen, and at Quyu and Yangwu against Yang Xiong, claiming twelve heads and ministerial grandee rank.

sentences.zh: 從至霸上。

translations.literal: He followed reaching Bashang.
translations.idiomatic: He marched with the host to Bashang.

sentences.zh: 沛公為漢王，賜寬封號共德君。

translations.literal: Lord Pei became King of Han; he granted Kuan the title Lord of shared virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Made King of Han, Lord Pei dubbed Kuan Lord of Shared Virtue.

sentences.zh: 從入漢中，為右騎將。

translations.literal: He followed in entering Hanzhong and was made right cavalry commander.
translations.idiomatic: He entered Hanzhong as right horse commander.

sentences.zh: 定三秦，賜食邑雕陰。

translations.literal: He settled the Three Qin and was granted a food fief at Diaoyin.
translations.idiomatic: The pacification of the Three Qin won him income at Diaoyin.

sentences.zh: 從擊項籍，待懷，賜爵通德侯。

translations.literal: He followed in striking Xiang Ji, waited at Huai, and was granted the rank Marquis of Pervading Virtue.
translations.idiomatic: He campaigned against Xiang Ji, held position at Huai, and received the title Marquis of Pervading Virtue.

sentences.zh: 從擊項冠、周蘭、龍且，所將卒斬騎將一人敖下，益食邑。

translations.literal: He followed in striking Xiang Guan, Zhou Lan, and Long Ju; the soldiers he led beheaded one cavalry general below Ao, and his food fief was increased.
translations.idiomatic: Against Xiang Guan, Zhou Lan, and Long Ju his men slew a horse commander below Ao and his fief grew again.

sentences.zh: 屬淮陰，擊破齊歷下軍，擊田解。

translations.literal: Attached to the Huaiyin commander he destroyed Qi’s army below Lixia and struck Tian Xie.
translations.idiomatic: Under the Huaiyin commander he broke the Qi host at Lixia and hit Tian Xie.

sentences.zh: 屬相國參，殘博，益食邑。

translations.literal: Attached to Chancellor of State Cao he ravaged Bo and his food fief was increased.
translations.idiomatic: Under Chancellor Cao he sacked Bo and gained another slice of fief.

sentences.zh: 因定齊地，剖符世世勿絕，封陽陵侯，二千六百戶，除前所食。

translations.literal: Thereupon settling Qi territory, tally-splitting perpetual through the generations, he was enfeoffed as Marquis of Yangling, two thousand six hundred households, rescinding his previous grants.
translations.idiomatic: He then helped finish Qi, received a perpetual split tally as Marquis of Yangling with twenty-six hundred households, superseding earlier grants.

sentences.zh: 為齊右丞相，備齊。

translations.literal: He was Qi’s right chancellor, guarding Qi.
translations.idiomatic: He served as right chancellor of Qi to hold the kingdom.

sentences.zh: 五歲為齊相國。

translations.literal: At five years he was Qi chancellor of state.
translations.idiomatic: For five years he was chancellor of state to the Qi king.

sentences.zh: 靳歙，以中涓從，起宛朐。

translations.literal: Jin She, as palace attendant follower, rose from Yuanqu.
translations.idiomatic: Jin She joined as palace attendant from the first muster at Yuanqu.

sentences.zh: 攻濟陽。

translations.literal: He attacked Jiyang.
translations.idiomatic: He stormed Jiyang.

sentences.zh: 破李由軍。

translations.literal: He destroyed Li You’s army.
translations.idiomatic: He shattered Li You’s force.

sentences.zh: 擊秦軍開封東，斬騎千人將一人，首五十七級，捕虜七十三人，賜爵封臨平君。

translations.literal: He struck Qin troops east of Kaifeng, beheaded one commander of a thousand horse, fifty-seven heads, captured seventy-three prisoners, and was granted a fief with the title Lord of Linping.
translations.idiomatic: East of Kaifeng he hit Qin columns, slew a commander of a thousand horse, took fifty-seven heads and seventy-three captives, and was named Lord of Linping.

sentences.zh: 又戰藍田北，斬車司馬二人，騎長一人，首二十八級，捕虜五十七人。

translations.literal: Again he fought north of Lantian, beheaded two chariot marshals, one cavalry chief, twenty-eight heads, and captured fifty-seven prisoners.
translations.idiomatic: North of Lantian he added two chariot marshals, a cavalry chief, twenty-eight heads, and fifty-seven prisoners.

sentences.zh: 至霸上。

translations.literal: He reached Bashang.
translations.idiomatic: He drove to Bashang.

sentences.zh: 沛公為漢王，賜歙爵建武侯，遷騎都尉。

translations.literal: Lord Pei became King of Han; he granted She the rank Marquis Who Establishes Martial Might and promoted him to chief of cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: Lord Pei’s kingship brought She the title Marquis Who Establishes Martial Might and promotion to chief of cavalry.

sentences.zh: 從定三秦。

translations.literal: He followed in settling the Three Qin.
translations.idiomatic: He took part in the reduction of the Three Qin.

sentences.zh: 別西擊章平軍於隴西，破之，定隴西六縣，所將卒斬車司馬、候各四人，騎長十二人。

translations.literal: Separately west he struck Zhang Ping’s army in Longxi, destroyed it, settled six counties of Longxi, and the soldiers he led beheaded four chariot marshals and four frontier captains each, twelve cavalry chiefs.
translations.idiomatic: Detaching west he broke Zhang Ping in Longxi, secured six counties there, and his men slew four chariot marshals, four captains (hou), and twelve cavalry chiefs.

sentences.zh: 從東擊楚，至彭城。

translations.literal: He followed east in striking Chu, reaching Pengcheng.
translations.idiomatic: He marched east against Chu as far as Pengcheng.

sentences.zh: 漢軍敗還，保雍丘，擊反者王武等。

translations.literal: The Han army was defeated and returned, guarding Yongqiu, striking the rebels Wang Wu and others.
translations.idiomatic: After the Han rout he helped hold Yongqiu and strike Wang Wu’s mutineers.

sentences.zh: 略梁地，別西擊邢說軍菑南，破之，身得說都尉二人，司馬、候十二人，降吏卒四千六百八十人。

translations.literal: He overran Liang territory; separately west he struck Xing Shuo’s army south of Zi, destroyed it, and personally obtained two commandants of Shuo, twelve marshals and marquises, and caused four thousand six hundred eighty officers and troops to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: He ranged Liang, then swung west to crush Xing Shuo south of Zi, personally seizing two of Shuo’s commandants and a dozen marshals while bringing in four thousand six hundred eighty men.

sentences.zh: 破楚軍滎陽東。

translations.literal: He destroyed Chu troops east of Xingyang.
translations.idiomatic: East of Xingyang he broke a Chu division.

sentences.zh: 食邑四千二百戶。

translations.literal: His food fief was four thousand two hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: His grant stood at forty-two hundred households.

sentences.zh: 別之河內，擊趙賁軍朝歌，破之，所將卒得騎將二人，車馬二百五十匹。

translations.literal: Separately he went to Henei, struck Zhao Fen’s army at Zhaoge, destroyed it, and the soldiers he led obtained two cavalry generals, two hundred fifty chariots and horses.
translations.idiomatic: He moved independently into Henei, smashed Zhao Fen at Zhaoge, and took two horse generals with two hundred fifty mounts.

sentences.zh: 從攻安陽以東，至棘蒲，下十縣。

translations.literal: He followed in attacking east of Anyang to Jipu, taking ten counties.
translations.idiomatic: He cleared ten counties from Anyang east to Jipu.

sentences.zh: 別攻破趙軍，得其將司馬二人，候四人，降吏卒二千四百人。

translations.literal: Separately he attacked and destroyed Zhao’s army, obtaining its two marshals, four marquises, and causing two thousand four hundred officers and troops to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: On his own he broke a Zhao army, capturing two marshals, four marquises, and twenty-four hundred men.

sentences.zh: 從降下邯鄲。

translations.literal: He followed in causing Handan to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: He helped force Handan to capitulate.

sentences.zh: 別下平陽，身斬守相，所將卒斬兵守郡一人，降鄴。

translations.literal: Separately he took Pingyang, personally beheaded the assistant governor, the soldiers he led beheaded one commandant who garrisoned the commandery, and caused Ye to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: He seized Pingyang, cut down the assistant governor with his own blade while his men slew a garrison commandant, then forced Ye to yield.

sentences.zh: 從攻朝歌、邯鄲，又別擊破趙郡，降邯鄲郡六縣。

translations.literal: He followed in attacking Zhaoge and Handan, again separately struck and destroyed Zhao commandery, and caused six counties of Handan commandery to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: He fought at Zhaoge and Handan, then detached to shatter Zhao commandery and win six Handan counties.

sentences.zh: 還軍敖倉，破項籍軍成皋南，擊絕楚饟道，起滎陽至襄邑。

translations.literal: Returning the army to Aocang he destroyed Xiang Ji’s army south of Chenggao, struck and cut Chu’s supply road from Xingyang to Xiangyi.
translations.idiomatic: Pulling back to Aocang he hit Xiang Ji south of Chenggao and severed Chu’s convoy line from Xingyang to Xiangyi.

sentences.zh: 破項冠魯下。

translations.literal: He destroyed Xiang Guan below Lu.
translations.idiomatic: Below Lu he smashed Xiang Guan.

sentences.zh: 略地東至觞、郯、下邳，南至蘄、竹邑。

translations.literal: He overran territory east to Zeng, Tan, and Xiapi, south to Qi and Zhuyi.
translations.idiomatic: His columns ranged east to Zeng, Tan, and Xiapi and south to Qi and Zhuyi.

sentences.zh: 擊項悍濟陽下。

translations.literal: He struck Xiang Han below Jiyang.
translations.idiomatic: He defeated Xiang Han beneath Jiyang.

sentences.zh: 還擊項籍軍陳下，破之。

translations.literal: Returning he struck Xiang Ji’s army below Chen and destroyed it.
translations.idiomatic: On the way back he broke Xiang Ji again beneath Chen.

sentences.zh: 別定江陵，降柱國、大司馬以下八人，身得江陵王，致雒陽，因定南郡。

translations.literal: Separately he settled Jiangling, caused pillar-of-state, grand marshal, and below eight men to surrender, personally obtained the King of Jiangling, conveyed him to Luoyang, and thereupon settled Nan commandery.
translations.idiomatic: He independently secured Jiangling, forced eight officers from pillar-of-state down to yield, escorted the King of Jiangling to Luoyang, and annexed Nan commandery.

sentences.zh: 從至陳，取楚王信，剖符世世勿絕，定食四千六百戶，為信武侯。

translations.literal: He followed to Chen, seized the King of Chu Xin, tally-splitting perpetual through the generations, settled food at four thousand six hundred households, and was Marquis Who Trusts in Martial Might.
translations.idiomatic: At Chen he helped arrest King Xin of Chu, took a perpetual split tally, fixed his fief at forty-six hundred households, and bore the title Marquis Who Trusts in Martial Might.

sentences.zh: 以騎都尉擊代，攻韓信平城下，還軍東垣。

translations.literal: As chief of cavalry he struck Dai, attacked Han Xin below Pingcheng, and returned the army to Dongyuan.
translations.idiomatic: As chief of cavalry he campaigned in Dai, struck Han Xin beneath Pingcheng, then drew the host back to Dongyuan.

sentences.zh: 有功，遷為車騎將軍，并將梁、趙、齊、燕、楚車騎，別擊陳豨丞相敞，破之，因降曲逆。

translations.literal: Having merit, he was promoted to chariot-and-cavalry general and jointly commanded Liang, Zhao, Qi, Yan, and Chu chariots and horse; separately he struck Chen Xi’s chancellor Chang, destroyed him, and thereupon caused Quni to surrender.
translations.idiomatic: Merit raised him to chariot-and-cavalry general over the horse of Liang, Zhao, Qi, Yan, and Chu; he detached against Chen Xi’s chancellor Chang, broke him, and took Quni.

sentences.zh: 從擊黥布有功，益封，定食邑五千三百戶。

translations.literal: Following in striking Qing Bu he had merit; his fief was increased and settled food fief was five thousand three hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: His part against Qing Bu brought further enfeoffment, fixing his grant at fifty-three hundred households.

sentences.zh: 凡斬首九十級，虜百四十二人，別破軍十四，降城五十九，定郡、國各一，縣二十三，得王、柱國各一人，二千石以下至五石三十九人。

translations.literal: In all he beheaded ninety grades, captured one hundred forty-two men, separately destroyed fourteen armies, caused fifty-nine cities to surrender, settled one commandery and one kingdom each, twenty-three counties, obtained one king and one pillar-of-state each, and thirty-nine men from two thousand piculs down to five hundred piculs.
translations.idiomatic: In sum he claimed ninety heads and one hundred forty-two prisoners, broke fourteen armies on his own, took fifty-nine towns, set one commandery and one kingdom and twenty-three counties to rights, captured a king and a pillar-of-state, and thirty-nine officers from two thousand piculs down to five hundred piculs.

sentences.zh: 高后五年，薨，諡曰肅侯。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Empress Gao he died and was posthumously titled Stern Marquis.
translations.idiomatic: He died in the fifth year of Empress Gao’s reign with the posthumous name Stern Marquis.

sentences.zh: 子亭嗣，有罪，國除。

translations.literal: His son Ting succeeded; for crime the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: His heir Ting inherited the title but lost the fief to a conviction.

sentences.zh: 周惞，沛人也。

translations.literal: Zhou Xun was a man of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: Zhou Xun came from Pei.

sentences.zh: 以舍人從高祖起沛。

translations.literal: As a house-guest retainer he followed the High Ancestor in rising from Pei.
translations.idiomatic: He joined the uprising as a house-guest at the High Ancestor’s side.

sentences.zh: 至霸上，西入蜀漢，還定三秦，常為參乘，賜食邑池陽。

translations.literal: He reached Bashang, westward entered Shu and Han, returned and settled the Three Qin, constantly was chariot-side guard, and was granted a food fief at Chiyang.
translations.idiomatic: He rode to Bashang, followed the march into Ba–Shu, turned back to pacify the Three Qin, habitually rode at the ruler’s right hand, and received income at Chiyang.

sentences.zh: 從東擊項羽滎陽，絕甬道，從出度平陰，遇韓信軍襄國，戰有利不利，終亡離上心。

translations.literal: He followed east in striking Xiang Yu at Xingyang, cut the covered corridor, followed in going out and crossing Pingyin, met Han Xin’s army at Xiangguo, in battle sometimes had advantage sometimes disadvantage, but in the end never lost the ruler’s favor.
translations.idiomatic: East of Xingyang he fought Xiang Yu, severed the supply gallery, crossed Pingyin on the retreat, and joined Han Xin at Xiangguo—wins and losses mixed, yet he never fell from the emperor’s trust.

sentences.zh: 上以惞為信武侯，食邑三千三百戶。

translations.literal: The emperor for Xun made him Marquis Who Trusts in Martial Might with a food fief of three thousand three hundred households.
translations.idiomatic: The throne ennobled him as Marquis Who Trusts in Martial Might with thirty-three hundred households.

sentences.zh: 上欲自擊陳豨，惞泣曰：「始秦攻破天下，未曾自行，今上常自行，是亡人可使者乎？」

translations.literal: When the emperor wished personally to strike Chen Xi, Xun wept and said: “When Qin first overran the realm its ruler never took the field in person; now Your Majesty constantly campaigns in person—does this mean there is no man you can send to do the work?”
translations.idiomatic: The emperor meant to lead the expedition against Chen Xi himself; Xun wept, “Even Qin at the height of conquest did not ride out on every campaign; if you must always go yourself, have you truly no commander left to dispatch?”

sentences.zh: 上以為「愛我」，賜入殿門不趨。

translations.literal: The emperor took it as “loving me” and granted him entry through the hall gate without quick-stepping.
translations.idiomatic: The sovereign read this as devotion and allowed him to enter the audience hall without the usual hurried gait.

sentences.zh: 十二年，更封惞為糨城侯，孝文五年薨，諡曰貞侯。

translations.literal: In the twelfth year he was transferred to enfeoffment as Marquis of Zhicheng; in the fifth year of Emperor Wen he died and was posthumously titled Faithful Marquis.
translations.idiomatic: In the twelfth year of his reign the emperor moved his patent to the marquisate of Zhicheng; he died in Emperor Wen’s fifth year with the posthumous name Faithful Marquis.

sentences.zh: 子昌嗣，有罪，國除。

translations.literal: His son Chang succeeded; for crime the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: His son Chang inherited but forfeited the fief for a crime.

sentences.zh: 景帝復封惞子應為鄲侯，薨，諡曰康侯。

translations.literal: Emperor Jing again enfeoffed Xun’s son Ying as Marquis of Dan; he died and was posthumously titled Vigorous Marquis.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Jing restored the line by making Xun’s son Ying Marquis of Dan; he died as Vigorous Marquis.

sentences.zh: 子仲居嗣，坐為太常有罪，國除。

translations.literal: His son Zhongju succeeded; for sitting as grand master of ceremonies with guilt the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Zhongju succeeded but lost the title when convicted in office as grand master of ceremonies.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：仲尼稱「犁牛之子騂且角，雖欲勿用，山川其舍諸？」

translations.literal: The appraising comment says: Confucius said, “The calf of a brindled cow is red-furred and horned; though you might wish not to use it, would the hills and streams leave it aside?”
translations.idiomatic: The summation quotes Confucius: “Though the brindled cow’s calf is sleek and horned, and you might hesitate to sacrifice it, would heaven and earth cast it away?”

sentences.zh: 言士不繫於世類也。

translations.literal: It speaks of scholars not being bound to their worldly class.
translations.idiomatic: —meaning talent is not chained to birth or station.

sentences.zh: 語曰「雖有茲基，不如逢時」，信矣！

translations.literal: The saying goes, “Though you have a hoe, it is not as good as meeting the right season”—how true!
translations.idiomatic: The proverb runs, “A fine mattock is nothing without the right season”—and it holds.

sentences.zh: 樊噲、夏侯嬰、灌嬰之徒，方其鼓刀僕御販繒之時，豈自知附驥之尾，勤功帝籍，慶流子孫哉？

translations.literal: Men such as Fan Kuai, Xiahou Ying, and Guan Ying—when they were wielding cleavers, driving carriages, or peddling silk, could they have known they would cling to the tail of a swift steed, toil their names into the imperial registers, and pass blessing down to their descendants?
translations.idiomatic: Fan Kuai, Xiahou Ying, Guan Ying—dog-butcher, driver, peddler—who could have guessed they would ride the coattails of empire, carve their deeds into the annals, and leave fortune to their heirs?

sentences.zh: 當孝文時，天下以酈寄為賣友。

translations.literal: In the time of Emperor Wen, all under Heaven called Li Ji a seller of friends.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Wen the realm branded Li Ji a traitor to friendship.

sentences.zh: 夫賣友者，謂見利而忘義也。

translations.literal: As for “selling friends,” it means seeing profit and forgetting righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: “Selling friends” is the charge of choosing gain over duty.

sentences.zh: 若寄父為功臣而又執劫，雖摧呂祿，以安社稷，誼存君親，可也。

translations.literal: Yet if Ji’s father was a founding minister who was taken hostage, and though he overthrew Lü Lu to secure the altars of soil and grain, his conduct still honored both sovereign and kin—that may be allowed.
translations.idiomatic: But when a meritorious father is held at sword-point, and destroying Lü Lu is what saves the dynasty, conduct that preserves ruler and family alike deserves indulgence.
